How to Use This Guide
You do not need any programming background to start this book. Every idea is built from zero, in order,
and nothing later depends on something you have not already seen. That is the deal: read it in order
the first time.
What this guide assumes
Nothing.  Not  C,  not  math  beyond  basic  arithmetic,  not  any  prior  data  structures  knowledge.  If  you
already know some C, skim Chapter 1 and jump to Chapter 2.
How each topic is taught
Every  topic  in  this  book  follows  the  same  four-step  loop,  because  this  is  the  loop  that  actually  builds
skill:
1.  Understand -- what problem does this idea solve, in plain words, before any code.
2.  Code -- a complete, compilable C implementation. Type it out yourself at least once. Typing
beats copy-pasting for building muscle memory.
3.  Trace -- a hand-run example, value by value, so you can see the idea move.
4.  Practice -- a short list of real problems (mostly from LeetCode) that use exactly this idea, so you
go from "I understand it" to "I can use it under time pressure."
How to actually get good at this
Reading  this  guide  will  not  make  you  good  at  solving  problems.  Typing  out  every  example  and  then
closing  the  book  and  solving  the  practice  problems  yourself  will.  When  you  get  stuck  on  a  practice
problem, that stuck feeling is the whole point -- it is your brain building the pattern. Give it 20-30 real
minutes before looking anything up.
A realistic path to placement-ready
Chapters  1-16  are  the  foundation  --  work  through  them  in  order  over  several  weeks.  Chapter  17  is
bonus material that shows up in harder interviews but is not required to start solving problems. Chapter
18 is a pattern playbook you will return to constantly once you start solving problems for real. Chapter
19 turns all of this into a week-by- week study plan. Chapter 20 is a cheat sheet to flip back to anytime
you forget a complexity or want a curated problem list for a topic.
Let's begin.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 6

1. C Programming Crash Course
C is the right first language for learning data structures and algorithms for one reason: it has no hidden
machinery. When you write a linked list in Python or Java, a lot of what makes it work is hidden from
you. In C, you build the node, you allocate its memory yourself, and you wire the pointers yourself. That
is  slower  at  first  and  much  better  for  actually  understanding  what  a  data  structure  is.  This  chapter
covers only what you need for the rest of the book -- it is not a complete C course.
1.1 Your first program and how to run it
#include <stdio.h>
int main() {
    printf("Hello, DSA!\n");
    return 0;
}
Save this as hello.c. On any Linux/Mac terminal, or on Windows with MinGW installed, compile and
run it with:
gcc hello.c -o hello
./hello
gcc is the compiler. It turns your .c text file into a binary the machine can actually run. -o hello names
the output binary hello. If you do not want to install anything yet, any online compiler (like the ones on
programiz.com or replit.com) works fine for the first few chapters.
#include  <stdio.h>  pulls  in  the  Standard  Input/Output  library,  which  is  where  printf  lives.  int
main() is the function every C program starts running from. return 0; tells the operating system the
program finished without errors.
1.2 Variables and data types
A  variable  is  a  named  box  in  memory.  In  C,  you  must  state  what  type  of  value  the  box  will  hold,
because C uses that information to know how many bytes to reserve and how to interpret them.
int age = 15;              // whole numbers, usually 4 bytes
float price = 99.50f;      // decimal numbers, 4 bytes, ~7 digit precision
double pi = 3.14159265;     // decimal numbers, 8 bytes, more precision
char grade = 'A';           // a single character, 1 byte
int typically ranges from about -2.1 billion to 2.1 billion. When a number can exceed that (like counting
something huge, or multiplying two large numbers), you will need long long, which typically gives you
about  -9.2  quintillion  to  9.2  quintillion.  This  matters  a  lot  in  interviews:  a  very  common  bug  is  an  int
overflowing  silently  when  two  large  numbers  are  multiplied.  When  in  doubt  for  competitive  problems,
use long long.
long long big = 10000000000LL;   // the LL suffix tells the compiler this is long long
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 7

1.3 Operators
int a = 7, b = 2;
int sum = a + b;        // 9
int diff = a - b;       // 5
int prod = a * b;       // 14
int quot = a / b;       // 3  -- integer division truncates toward zero
int rem = a % b;        // 1  -- modulo, the remainder after division
Comparison  operators  (<,  >,  <=,  >=,  ==,  !=)  produce  1  (true)  or  0  (false)  in  C  --  there  is  no  separate
boolean type in classic C (C99 added _Bool, but you will mostly just use int for true/false).
Logical operators: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT). These short-circuit: in a() && b(), if a() returns false,
b() is never called.
1.4 Control flow
if (age >= 18) {
    printf("Adult\n");
} else if (age >= 13) {
    printf("Teen\n");
} else {
    printf("Child\n");
}
int i = 0;
while (i < 5) {
    printf("%d ", i);
    i++;
}
for (int j = 0; j < 5; j++) {
    printf("%d ", j);
}
int k = 0;
do {
    printf("%d ", k);
    k++;
} while (k < 5);
for (init; condition; update) is the workhorse of DSA code -- almost every array traversal in this
book uses it. Read it as: "do init once, then while condition is true, run the body, then run update,
and check condition again."
1.5 Functions
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 8

int add(int x, int y) {
    return x + y;
}
int main() {
    int result = add(3, 4);
    printf("%d\n", result);   // 7
    return 0;
}
C  is  call-by-value  by  default:  when  you  pass  x  into  a  function,  the  function  gets  a  copy.  Changing  x
inside  the  function  does  not  change  the  original  variable  in  the  caller.  This  is  exactly  why  pointers
(section 1.7) matter so much -- they are how you let a function actually modify something back in the
caller.
1.6 Arrays
An  array  is  a  block  of  memory  holding  many  values  of  the  same  type,  laid  out  back  to  back,  so  the
computer can jump straight to any index in constant time.
int nums[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    printf("%d ", nums[i]);
}
nums[2] = 99;   // overwrite index 2 -> {10, 20, 99, 40, 50}
nums[i] is actually shorthand for "go to the start of nums, move i slots forward, and read that value."
Because  each  int  takes  a  fixed  number  of  bytes,  the  computer  can  calculate  exactly  where  index  i
lives without scanning anything -- that is why array access is O(1).
Two-dimensional arrays are just arrays of arrays:
int grid[3][4];   // 3 rows, 4 columns
for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) {
    for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++) {
        grid[r][c] = r * 4 + c;
    }
}
C arrays do not know their own length -- unlike Python lists, a C array has no .length. You must track
the  size  yourself  in  a  separate  variable,  almost  always.  This  is  one  of  the  most  common  sources  of
bugs for beginners: forgetting to pass the size alongside the array into a function.
1.7 Pointers -- the most important idea in this chapter
A pointer is a variable that stores a memory address instead of a value. This sounds abstract, so build
the mental picture directly: imagine memory as a huge street of numbered houses. A normal variable is
a house with a value inside. A pointer is a house whose value is the address of another house.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 9

int x = 10;
int *p = &x;    // p now holds the address of x
printf("%d\n", x);     // 10          -- the value
printf("%p\n", (void*)&x);  // 0x7ffee... -- the address of x
printf("%p\n", (void*)p);   // 0x7ffee... -- same address, stored in p
printf("%d\n", *p);    // 10          -- "dereference" p: go to that address, read it
*p = 99;
printf("%d\n", x);     // 99  -- changing *p changed x, because p points AT x
Three symbols to lock in:
•  &x means "the address of x." Read & as "address-of."
•  int *p means "p is a pointer to an int." Read the * here as "pointer to."
•  *p (outside a declaration) means "go to the address p holds, and read/write there." Read this * as
"dereference."
This is exactly how you make call-by-value functions modify the caller's variable:
void increment(int *p) {
    *p = *p + 1;
}
int main() {
    int x = 5;
    increment(&x);
    printf("%d\n", x);   // 6 -- because increment received x's address, not a copy
    return 0;
}
Pointer arithmetic and arrays
An  array's  name,  used  by  itself,  decays  into  a  pointer  to  its  first  element.  This  is  why  nums[i]  and
*(nums + i) mean the same thing:
int nums[3] = {10, 20, 30};
printf("%d\n", nums[1]);        // 20
printf("%d\n", *(nums + 1));    // 20 -- identical
nums + 1 does not mean "add 1 byte" -- it means "move forward by 1 int's worth of bytes," whatever
that is on your machine (usually 4). The compiler works this out from the pointer's type.
Pointer to pointer
You  will  meet  Node** type  patterns  a  lot  once  you  reach  linked  lists,  because  sometimes  a  function
needs to modify what a pointer itself points to (not just the value it points to) -- for example, changing
the head of a list from inside a helper function.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 10

void setToFive(int **pp) {
    **pp = 5;
}
int main() {
    int x = 1;
    int *p = &x;
    setToFive(&p);
    printf("%d\n", x);   // 5
    return 0;
}
1.8 Strings in C
C  has  no  built-in  string  type  --  a  string  is  just  an  array  of  char  that  ends  with  a  special  null  character
'\0' (byte value 0), which marks "the string stops here."
char name[6] = "hello";   // {'h','e','l','l','o','\0'}  -- 6 bytes, not 5
You  need  one  extra  byte  of  room  for  the  terminator,  every  time.  Forgetting  this  is  a  classic  source  of
buffer overflow bugs. Common string functions (from <string.h>):
#include <string.h>
char a[20] = "hello";
char b[20] = "world";
strlen(a);          // 5  -- length NOT counting '\0'
strcpy(a, b);        // copies b into a -- a is now "world"
strcat(a, b);        // appends b onto a
strcmp(a, b);        // 0 if equal, <0 if a<b alphabetically, >0 if a>b
For DSA problems, you will often treat a string as char arr[] and index into it like any array (arr[i]),
scanning with strlen(arr) as the bound.
1.9 Structures
A struct bundles several related variables under one name. This is how you build every custom data
structure in this book -- nodes, tree cells, graph edges, all of them are structs.
struct Point {
    int x;
    int y;
};
int main() {
    struct Point p1;
    p1.x = 3;
    p1.y = 4;
    printf("(%d, %d)\n", p1.x, p1.y);
    return 0;
}
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 11

Writing struct Point everywhere is repetitive, so typedef gives the type a shorter alias -- you will see
this pattern in almost every code block in this book:
typedef struct {
    int x;
    int y;
} Point;
Point p1 = {3, 4};
When  you  have  a  pointer  to  a  struct,  use  ->  instead  of  .  to  reach  its  fields  --  ->  is  shorthand  for
"dereference, then access a field":
Point *pp = &p1;
printf("%d\n", pp->x);      // same as (*pp).x
1.10 Dynamic memory allocation
Every array or struct you have seen so far lives on the "stack" -- memory that is reserved automatically
when a function starts and freed automatically when it returns. The stack cannot grow a data structure
at runtime, and it disappears the moment the function ends. Data structures like linked lists, trees, and
graphs  need  memory  that  survives  beyond  a  single  function  call  and  can  grow  as  needed  --  that
memory comes from the "heap," and you manage it yourself using <stdlib.h>.
#include <stdlib.h>
int *arr = (int *) malloc(5 * sizeof(int));   // room for 5 ints, uninitialized
if (arr == NULL) {
    // malloc failed -- always check this in real code
}
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    arr[i] = i * i;
}
free(arr);    // give the memory back -- REQUIRED, or it is a memory leak
arr = NULL;   // good practice: avoid an accidental "dangling pointer" reuse
•  malloc(n) reserves n bytes and returns a pointer to the start, uninitialized (garbage values).
sizeof(int) gives the exact byte size of an int on your machine, so malloc(5 * sizeof(int))
reads as "room for 5 ints."
•  calloc(n, size) does the same but zero-initializes the memory.
•  realloc(ptr, newSize) grows or shrinks a previous allocation, copying old data over
automatically.
•  free(ptr) returns memory to the system. Every malloc/calloc must eventually be matched with
exactly one free. Forgetting this is a memory leak; calling free twice on the same pointer, or using
memory after freeing it, is undefined behavior and a classic interview red flag if you do it live.
This is the single mechanism that makes linked lists, trees, and graphs possible in C: every "node" you
create in this entire book is a malloc'd struct, wired to other malloc'd structs by pointers.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 12

1.11 Recursion, in brief (full chapter later)
A recursive function is one that calls itself on a smaller version of the same problem, until it hits a case
simple enough to answer directly (the "base case").
int factorial(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;         // base case
    return n * factorial(n - 1);   // recursive case: smaller problem
}
Every recursive call gets its own space on the call stack, holding its own copy of n. factorial(4) waits
on  4 * factorial(3),  which  waits  on  3 * factorial(2),  and  so  on,  until  factorial(1)  returns  1
directly  and  the  multiplications  unwind  back  up.  Chapter  5  goes  deep  on  this;  for  now,  just  recognize
the shape: base case checked first, recursive case calls the same function on a smaller input.
1.12 Common beginner mistakes (that also show up in interviews)
•  Segmentation fault ("segfault"): reading or writing memory you do not own -- most often,
dereferencing a NULL pointer, or an uninitialized pointer, or reading past the end of an array.
•  Off-by-one errors: looping i <= n when it should be i < n, or the reverse. Always double check
your loop bound against a size-1 array by hand.
•  Memory leaks: malloc without a matching free. Not fatal in a 5-minute LeetCode run, but
interviewers notice if you never free anything you allocate.
•  Comparing strings with ==: str1 == str2 compares addresses, not contents. Always use
strcmp(str1, str2) == 0 for content comparison.
•  Integer overflow: adding two large ints that exceed about 2.1 billion wraps around silently
instead of erroring. Use long long when values could get large.
•  Uninitialized variables: an int x; with no assignment holds garbage, not 0. Always initialize
before reading.
With this foundation, you have everything you need to build every data structure in this book. Chapter 2
covers how to reason about how fast your code is -- the language every interview and every LeetCode
difficulty rating is written in.

2. Complexity Analysis -- Big-O and Friends
2.1 Why this chapter matters more than it looks like it does
Every LeetCode problem has hidden constraints, like "1 <= n <= 100000." Those numbers are a direct
hint  about  which  complexity  is  required.  If  n  can  be  100,000,  an  O(n^2)  solution  will  run  roughly
10,000,000,000  basic  operations  --  far  too  slow,  and  your  submission  will  fail  with  "Time  Limit
Exceeded" even if the logic is perfectly correct. Learning to read those constraints and match them to a
complexity class is one of the highest-leverage skills in this entire book.
2.2 What Big-O actually measures
Big-O describes how the runtime (or memory) of an algorithm grows as the input size n grows, ignoring
constant  factors  and  lower-order  terms.  It  answers  "if  I  double  n,  roughly  what  happens  to  the  work
done?" -- not "how many milliseconds will this take," which depends on the machine.
•  O(1) -- constant: work does not depend on n at all. Array index access.
•  O(log n) -- logarithmic: work grows very slowly. Binary search: each step cuts the remaining
space in half, so 1,000,000 items take only about 20 steps.
•  O(n) -- linear: work grows proportionally with n. A single pass over an array.
•  O(n log n) -- linearithmic: the complexity of the best comparison-based sorts (merge sort,
quicksort on average, heap sort).
•  O(n^2) -- quadratic: nested loops over the same input. Bubble sort. Fine for small n (a few
thousand), too slow for large n.
•  O(2^n) -- exponential: doubles with every additional element. Naive recursive subset generation.
Only survives tiny n (up to about 20-25).
•  O(n!) -- factorial: generating every permutation. Only survives truly tiny n (up to about 10-11).

2.4 Reading complexity directly out of code
A single loop over n items is O(n):
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    printf("%d\n", arr[i]);   // O(1) work, done n times -> O(n)
}
Nested loops multiply:
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
        printf("%d %d\n", i, j);   // O(1) work, done n * n times -> O(n^2)
    }
}
A loop that halves its range each time is O(log n):
int i = n;
while (i > 1) {
    i = i / 2;     // this line runs about log2(n) times total
}
Two separate (non-nested) loops add, and Big-O keeps only the largest term:
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { }      // O(n)
for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {         // O(n)
    for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) { }   // nested -> O(n^2)
}
// total: O(n) + O(n^2) = O(n^2)  -- the n^2 term dominates, so the n term is dropped
This  "drop  the  smaller  terms"  rule  is  exactly  what  Big-O  means:  we  care  about  the  dominant  growth
rate, not the exact operation count.
2.5 Recursive complexity, briefly
For  a  recursive  function,  complexity  is  usually:  (number  of  calls)  x  (work  per  call,  excluding  further
recursive  calls).  factorial(n)  makes  n  calls,  each  doing  O(1)  extra  work,  so  it  is  O(n).  A  recursive
function that makes 2 calls on n-1 each time (like naive Fibonacci) roughly doubles its call count each
level down, giving O(2^n). Chapter 5 and Chapter 15 return to this with recursion trees, which make it
visual.
2.6 Big-O vs Big-Omega vs Big-Theta
These three describe upper, lower, and tight bounds on growth:
•  Big-O (O): an upper bound -- "this algorithm never does more than roughly this much work." This
is what people mean colloquially when they say "the complexity is O(n)."
•  Big-Omega (Omega): a lower bound -- "this algorithm always does at least roughly this much
work."
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 15

•  Big-Theta (Theta): a tight bound -- both the upper and lower bound match, meaning the
algorithm's growth rate is exactly this, not just bounded by it.
In  interviews,  people  almost  always  say  "O(...)"  even  when  they  technically  mean  Theta.  You  will  not
lose points for doing the same -- just know the distinction exists if it comes up.
2.7 Space complexity
Space complexity measures extra memory used, beyond the input itself, as a function of n. A function
that uses a fixed number of extra variables regardless of input size is O(1) extra space ("in-place"). A
function that builds a new array of size n is O(n) extra space. A recursive function also spends O(depth)
space  on  the  call  stack,  even  if  it  allocates  no  extra  arrays  --  this  is  easy  to  forget  and  interviewers
specifically probe for it ("what is the space complexity, including the call stack?").
2.8 A note on amortized complexity
Some operations are usually cheap but occasionally expensive, and "amortized" complexity averages
that out over many calls. The classic example: a dynamic array (like a resizable array) that doubles its
capacity  when  full.  Any  single  append  that  triggers  a  resize  costs  O(n)  that  one  time,  but  because
doublings become exponentially rarer, the average cost per append across many calls is O(1). You will
see "amortized O(1)" used for exactly this kind of operation.
With a working vocabulary for "how fast," you are ready to start on real data structures. We begin with
the simplest one you already met in Chapter 1: the array, and the two techniques built directly on top of
it that show up constantly on LeetCode.

3. Arrays and Strings
3.1 What you already know, and what is new here
Chapter 1 covered array mechanics. This chapter covers the patterns built on top of arrays that solve a
huge  fraction  of  "Easy"  and  "Medium"  LeetCode  problems:  two  pointers,  sliding  window,  and  prefix
sums.  Learning  these  three  patterns  well  is  worth  more  than  memorizing  a  hundred  individual
problems, because each pattern instantly solves dozens of problems that look different on the surface
but share the same shape.

3.3 The two-pointer technique
The  idea:  instead  of  nested  loops  (O(n^2)),  use  two  index  variables  that  move  through  the  array
according to a rule, so the whole array is covered in a single pass (O(n)). This works especially well on
sorted arrays.
Example: does a sorted array contain two numbers that sum to a target?
#include <stdbool.h>
bool hasPairWithSum(int arr[], int n, int target) {
    int left = 0, right = n - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        int sum = arr[left] + arr[right];
        if (sum == target) return true;
        else if (sum < target) left++;   // need a bigger sum -> move left up
        else right--;                     // need a smaller sum -> move right down
    }
    return false;
}
Trace on arr = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9], target = 12:

Why this works: because the array is sorted, if the current sum is too small, moving right down can
only  make  it  smaller  too  --  the  only  move  that  can  increase  the  sum  is  moving  left  up.  That  single
insight is what turns an O(n^2) nested search into an O(n) sweep.
Another classic use -- reversing an array in place:
void reverseArray(int arr[], int n) {
    int left = 0, right = n - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        int temp = arr[left];
        arr[left] = arr[right];
        arr[right] = temp;
        left++;
        right--;
    }
}
And checking a palindrome:
bool isPalindrome(char s[], int n) {
    int left = 0, right = n - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        if (s[left] != s[right]) return false;
        left++;
        right--;
    }
    return true;
}
3.4 The sliding window technique
The  idea:  for  problems  about  a  contiguous  subarray  or  substring  (max  sum,  longest  run,  smallest
window meeting a condition), avoid recomputing from scratch for every possible window. Instead keep
a  window  defined  by  two  pointers,  and  when  the  window  slides  forward  by  one,  only  adjust  for  the
single element entering and the single element leaving.
Example: maximum sum of any subarray of a fixed size k
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 18

int maxSumSubarray(int arr[], int n, int k) {
    int windowSum = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
        windowSum += arr[i];   // sum of the first window
    }
    int maxSum = windowSum;
    for (int i = k; i < n; i++) {
        windowSum += arr[i] - arr[i - k];   // add new element, drop the old one
        if (windowSum > maxSum) maxSum = windowSum;
    }
    return maxSum;
}
This  is  O(n)  instead  of  the  naive  O(n*k)  you  would  get  by  summing  each  window  from  scratch  --
because each element is added exactly once and removed exactly once across the whole run.
A variable-size window -- longest substring without repeating characters:
int lengthOfLongestSubstring(char s[]) {
    int n = strlen(s);
    int lastSeen[256];
    for (int i = 0; i < 256; i++) lastSeen[i] = -1;  // -1 = not seen yet
    int start = 0, best = 0;
    for (int end = 0; end < n; end++) {
        char c = s[end];
        if (lastSeen[(unsigned char)c] >= start) {
            start = lastSeen[(unsigned char)c] + 1;   // shrink window past the repeat
        }
        lastSeen[(unsigned char)c] = end;
        int windowLen = end - start + 1;
        if (windowLen > best) best = windowLen;
    }
    return best;
}
Here end always moves forward; start only ever jumps forward too, never backward -- so despite the
nested-looking logic, every character is visited a bounded number of times, and the whole thing is O(n).
3.5 Prefix sums
The idea: if you will be asked "what is the sum of elements from index i to j?" many times, precompute
a running total once, so each individual query becomes O(1) instead of O(n).
void buildPrefixSum(int arr[], int n, int prefix[]) {
    prefix[0] = arr[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        prefix[i] = prefix[i - 1] + arr[i];
    }
}
// sum of arr[i..j] inclusive, using the prefix array:
int rangeSum(int prefix[], int i, int j) {
    if (i == 0) return prefix[j];
    return prefix[j] - prefix[i - 1];
}
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 19

Trace on arr = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]: prefix = [2, 6, 12, 20, 30]. Sum of indices 1 to 3 (4+6+8=18)
is prefix[3] - prefix[0] = 20 - 2 = 18. Correct, and O(1) per query after the one-time O(n) build.
3.6 Strings as arrays
Every technique above applies directly to strings, since a C string is just a char array. The one extra
thing  to  watch  for  is  the  null  terminator  '\0'  --  always  bound  your  loops  with  strlen(s),  computed
once and stored, rather than calling strlen inside the loop condition (which would silently turn an O(n)
loop into O(n^2), since strlen itself is an O(n) scan).
// WRONG (accidentally O(n^2)): strlen(s) recomputed every iteration
for (int i = 0; i < strlen(s); i++) { }
// RIGHT (O(n)): length computed once
int len = strlen(s);
for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) { }

4. Searching and Sorting
4.1 Linear search
The simplest possible search: check every element until you find a match or run out of elements. O(n)
time,  O(1)  space.  There  is  no  faster  way  to  search  an  unsorted  array  --  you  cannot  skip  elements
without risking skipping the answer.
int linearSearch(int arr[], int n, int target) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (arr[i] == target) return i;
    }
    return -1;
}
4.2 Binary search
If the array is sorted, you can do dramatically better than linear search by repeatedly cutting the search
space in half.
int binarySearch(int arr[], int n, int target) {
    int lo = 0, hi = n - 1;
    while (lo <= hi) {
        int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;   // avoids overflow vs (lo+hi)/2
        if (arr[mid] == target) return mid;
        else if (arr[mid] < target) lo = mid + 1;
        else hi = mid - 1;
    }
    return -1;
}
Trace on arr = [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14], target = 10:
lohimidarr[mid]action
06388 < 10, lo = 4
4651212 > 10, hi = 4
44410match, return 4
Three answers in a 7-element array -- and that ratio only improves as n grows: 20 comparisons handle
a million elements. This is why "can this be sorted, or is it already sorted?" is one of the first questions
worth asking on any search problem.
Two bugs to specifically avoid:
•  Writing mid = (lo + hi) / 2 — for very large arrays this can overflow before the division
happens. lo + (hi - lo) / 2 avoids that.
•  Off-by-one errors in updating lo/hi — always mid + 1 and mid - 1, never just mid, or you risk an
infinite loop.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 21

4.3 Binary search variants
Find the first occurrence of a value in a sorted array with duplicates:
int firstOccurrence(int arr[], int n, int target) {
    int lo = 0, hi = n - 1, result = -1;
    while (lo <= hi) {
        int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
        if (arr[mid] == target) {
            result = mid;
            hi = mid - 1;         // keep searching to the LEFT for an earlier match
        } else if (arr[mid] < target) {
            lo = mid + 1;
        } else {
            hi = mid - 1;
        }
    }
    return result;
}
The trick generalizes: instead of returning the instant you find a match, record it and keep narrowing in
the direction you need (left for "first," right for "last"). This same "narrow toward a condition" idea also
solves an entire class of problems known as "binary search on the answer" -- for example, "find the
minimum  capacity  that  lets  you  ship  all  packages  within  D  days"  --  where  you  binary  search  over
possible  answers  rather  than  over  array  indices,  checking  at  each  midpoint  whether  that  answer  is
feasible.
Search in a rotated sorted array: at every step, at least one half of the current range (left-of-mid or
right-of-mid) is guaranteed to still be sorted. Check which half is sorted, then check if the target lies in
that half's range; if so recurse there, if not recurse in the other half. It is still O(log n) because you still
halve the space every step -- you just need one extra comparison to figure out which half is clean.
4.4 Sorting algorithms
Bubble sort -- O(n^2), for understanding, not for use
void bubbleSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n - 1 - i; j++) {
            if (arr[j] > arr[j + 1]) {
                int temp = arr[j];
                arr[j] = arr[j + 1];
                arr[j + 1] = temp;
            }
        }
    }
}
Repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order pairs, so the largest remaining value "bubbles" to the end each
full pass. Simple to reason about, never fast enough for real use past tiny inputs.
Selection sort -- O(n^2)
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 22

void selectionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        int minIdx = i;
        for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < arr[minIdx]) minIdx = j;
        }
        int temp = arr[i];
        arr[i] = arr[minIdx];
        arr[minIdx] = temp;
    }
}
Finds the minimum of the unsorted remainder and places it at the front, one position at a time.
Insertion sort -- O(n^2), but genuinely useful for small or nearly-sorted data
void insertionSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        int key = arr[i];
        int j = i - 1;
        while (j >= 0 && arr[j] > key) {
            arr[j + 1] = arr[j];   // shift right to make room
            j--;
        }
        arr[j + 1] = key;
    }
}
Builds up a sorted prefix one element at a time, inserting each new element into its correct place, like
sorting playing cards in your hand.
Merge sort -- O(n log n), stable, needs O(n) extra space
void merge(int arr[], int lo, int mid, int hi) {
    int n1 = mid - lo + 1, n2 = hi - mid;
    int left[n1], right[n2];
    for (int i = 0; i < n1; i++) left[i] = arr[lo + i];
    for (int j = 0; j < n2; j++) right[j] = arr[mid + 1 + j];
    int i = 0, j = 0, k = lo;
    while (i < n1 && j < n2) {
        if (left[i] <= right[j]) arr[k++] = left[i++];
        else arr[k++] = right[j++];
    }
    while (i < n1) arr[k++] = left[i++];
    while (j < n2) arr[k++] = right[j++];
}
void mergeSort(int arr[], int lo, int hi) {
    if (lo >= hi) return;              // base case: 0 or 1 element is sorted
    int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
    mergeSort(arr, lo, mid);           // sort left half
    mergeSort(arr, mid + 1, hi);       // sort right half
    merge(arr, lo, mid, hi);           // merge the two sorted halves
}
Merge sort is the textbook "divide and conquer" algorithm: split the array in half, recursively sort each
half, then merge the two sorted halves in linear time. Because the array halves every level, there are
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 23

log n levels, and each level does O(n) total work merging -- giving the O(n log n) you will see over
and over as the ceiling for comparison-based sorting.
Quicksort -- O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst case, in-place
int partition(int arr[], int lo, int hi) {
    int pivot = arr[hi];      // choosing the last element as pivot
    int i = lo - 1;
    for (int j = lo; j < hi; j++) {
        if (arr[j] < pivot) {
            i++;
            int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp;
        }
    }
    int temp = arr[i + 1]; arr[i + 1] = arr[hi]; arr[hi] = temp;
    return i + 1;             // final index of the pivot
}
void quickSort(int arr[], int lo, int hi) {
    if (lo < hi) {
        int p = partition(arr, lo, hi);
        quickSort(arr, lo, p - 1);
        quickSort(arr, p + 1, hi);
    }
}
Quicksort picks a pivot, partitions the array so everything smaller is on the left and everything bigger is
on  the  right,  then  recursively  sorts  each  side.  Its  worst  case  (O(n^2))  happens  when  the  pivot  choice
keeps being the smallest or largest remaining element (e.g., an already-sorted array with last-element
pivoting) -- production sorting libraries avoid this with randomized or median-of-three pivot selection.
Counting sort -- O(n + k), for a known small range of integers
void countingSort(int arr[], int n, int maxVal) {
    int count[maxVal + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= maxVal; i++) count[i] = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) count[arr[i]]++;
    int idx = 0;
    for (int v = 0; v <= maxVal; v++) {
        while (count[v] > 0) {
            arr[idx++] = v;
            count[v]--;
        }
    }
}
Skips comparisons entirely by counting how many times each value appears, then rebuilding the array
in  order.  Only  works  when  values  are  integers  in  a  known,  reasonably  small  range  k  --  it  is  not  a
general-purpose sort, but when it applies it beats the O(n log n) floor that comparison sorts are stuck
with.

4.6 When to use which
In an interview, C's standard library qsort() or your language's built-in sort is almost always what you
actually  call  --  you  are  rarely  asked  to  hand-write  a  full  sort  from  scratch.  What  you  are  expected  to
know:  merge  sort  and  heap  sort  guarantee  O(n  log  n)  even  in  the  worst  case,  quicksort  is  usually
fastest  in  practice  but  has  a  bad  worst  case,  and  insertion  sort  is  genuinely  the  right  choice  for  very
small or nearly-sorted arrays. Counting/radix sort are worth reaching for specifically when the problem
says values are bounded integers and n is large.

5. Recursion and Backtracking
5.1 Recursion, properly this time
A  recursive  function  solves  a  problem  by  solving  a  smaller  instance  of  the  same  problem,  and
combining that result. Every recursive function needs exactly two parts:
•  Base case: the smallest input, answered directly, with no further recursion. Without this, the
function calls itself forever (a "stack overflow" crash).
•  Recursive case: expresses the problem in terms of a smaller version of itself.
5.2 Seeing the call stack: the Fibonacci recursion tree
int fib(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;             // base case
    return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2);   // recursive case
}
Draw out fib(4) as a tree of calls, and something important becomes visible:
                fib(4)
               /      \
          fib(3)        fib(2)
         /      \        /    \
     fib(2)   fib(1)  fib(1) fib(0)
    /    \
fib(1)  fib(0)
fib(2) is computed twice, fib(1) is computed three times. This wasted, repeated work is exactly why
naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2^n) -- and exactly the problem that Dynamic Programming (Chapter 15)
exists  to  fix,  by  remembering  answers  instead  of  recomputing  them.  Keep  this  tree  in  mind;  it  is  the
single clearest picture of "why DP" you will encounter in this book.
5.3 Tail recursion, briefly
A recursive call is "tail recursive" if it is the very last thing the function does, with no pending work after
it returns (like the multiplication in factorial, which is not tail recursive, since n * still has to happen
after  the  recursive  call  returns).  Some  languages  optimize  tail  calls  into  a  loop  internally  to  avoid
growing the call stack; standard C compilers generally do not guarantee this optimization, so very deep
tail-recursive C code can still overflow the stack. For DSA interview purposes, know the term, but do not
rely on tail-call optimization in C.
5.4 Backtracking: recursion with undo
Backtracking solves problems by exploring choices one at a time, going deeper with each choice, and
undoing a choice to try the next alternative when a path does not lead anywhere useful (or once you
have recorded it as a valid answer). The pattern is always the same shape:
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 26

explore(state):
    if state is a complete valid answer:
        record it
        return
    for each choice available from state:
        make the choice           (change state)
        explore(new state)         (recurse)
        undo the choice            (restore state -- THIS is "backtracking")
The  "undo"  step  is  what  makes  it  backtracking  rather  than  plain  recursion:  after  trying  a  branch,  you
restore the exact state you had before, so the next iteration of the loop starts from a clean slate.
5.5 Subsets (the power set)
Every  element  has  exactly  two  choices:  include  it,  or  don't.  Trying  both  choices  for  every  element,
recursively, generates every possible subset.
int arr[] = {1, 2, 3};
int n = 3;
int current[10], currentSize = 0;
void printSubset() {
    printf("{ ");
    for (int i = 0; i < currentSize; i++) printf("%d ", current[i]);
    printf("}\n");
}
void subsets(int index) {
    if (index == n) {
        printSubset();
        return;
    }
    subsets(index + 1);                  // choice 1: exclude arr[index]
    current[currentSize++] = arr[index]; // choice 2: include arr[index]
    subsets(index + 1);
    currentSize--;                        // undo -- backtrack
}
For n = 3 this produces all 2^3 = 8 subsets, because each of the 3 elements doubles the number of
branches.
5.6 Permutations
Every position in the output can be filled by any element not yet used. Track which elements are used,
try each unused one at every position, and undo after each try.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 27

#include <stdbool.h>
int arr[] = {1, 2, 3};
int n = 3;
int current[10];
bool used[10] = {false};
void printPermutation() {
    printf("{ ");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) printf("%d ", current[i]);
    printf("}\n");
}
void permute(int depth) {
    if (depth == n) {
        printPermutation();
        return;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (used[i]) continue;
        used[i] = true;
        current[depth] = arr[i];
        permute(depth + 1);
        used[i] = false;   // undo -- backtrack
    }
}
5.7 N-Queens
Place one queen per row, choosing which column in that row is safe (no other queen shares its column
or  either  diagonal).  If  no  column  in  the  current  row  is  safe,  the  loop  simply  ends  with  nothing  placed,
and  the  function  returns  --  causing  the  caller  (the  previous  row)  to  try  its  next  column  option.  That
automatic  unwinding,  with  no  explicit  "undo"  line  needed  here,  is  still  backtracking:  each  call's  local
col[row] is naturally overwritten if a later attempt reuses that row.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 28

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#define MAXN 20
int col[MAXN], n;
int solutionCount = 0;
bool isSafe(int row, int c) {
    for (int r = 0; r < row; r++) {
        if (col[r] == c) return false;                     // same column
        if (abs(col[r] - c) == abs(r - row)) return false;  // same diagonal
    }
    return true;
}
void solveNQueens(int row) {
    if (row == n) {
        solutionCount++;
        return;
    }
    for (int c = 0; c < n; c++) {
        if (isSafe(row, c)) {
            col[row] = c;
            solveNQueens(row + 1);
        }
    }
}
Sudoku  Solver  uses  the  identical  shape:  try  a  digit  1-9  in  the  next  empty  cell,  check  row/column/box
validity instead of column/diagonal validity, recurse, and let the loop naturally move on to the next digit if
a branch fails.
5.8 Why backtracking is often exponential, and why that is
sometimes fine
Subsets  are  O(2^n),  permutations  are  O(n!)  --  both  grow  explosively.  This  is  fine  when  the  problem's
constraints say n is small (look back at the Chapter 2 table: n up to about 20-25 for O(2^n), up to about
10-11  for  O(n!)).  Recognizing  "small  n  in  the  constraints"  is  often  the  single  biggest  hint  that  a
backtracking solution is exactly what is expected, rather than a red flag that your approach is wrong.

6. Linked Lists
6.1 Why linked lists exist
Arrays are fast to index but slow to insert/delete in the middle, because everything after the gap must
shift. A linked list fixes this by giving up O(1) indexing in exchange for O(1) insertion/deletion once you
already have a pointer to the spot -- each element ("node") simply points to the next one, wherever it
happens to live in memory, so inserting a node is just rewiring two pointers, no shifting required.
typedef struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
} Node;
6.2 Core operations
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 30

#include <stdlib.h>
Node* createNode(int val) {
    Node* newNode = (Node*) malloc(sizeof(Node));
    newNode->data = val;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    return newNode;
}
Node* insertAtHead(Node* head, int val) {
    Node* newNode = createNode(val);
    newNode->next = head;
    return newNode;              // the new node becomes the head
}
Node* insertAtTail(Node* head, int val) {
    Node* newNode = createNode(val);
    if (head == NULL) return newNode;
    Node* temp = head;
    while (temp->next != NULL) temp = temp->next;
    temp->next = newNode;
    return head;
}
Node* deleteValue(Node* head, int val) {
    if (head == NULL) return NULL;
    if (head->data == val) {
        Node* toDelete = head;
        head = head->next;
        free(toDelete);
        return head;
    }
    Node* temp = head;
    while (temp->next != NULL && temp->next->data != val) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    if (temp->next != NULL) {
        Node* toDelete = temp->next;
        temp->next = temp->next->next;
        free(toDelete);
    }
    return head;
}
void printList(Node* head) {
    Node* temp = head;
    while (temp != NULL) {
        printf("%d -> ", temp->data);
        temp = temp->next;
    }
    printf("NULL\n");
}
Note that insertAtHead and deleteValue both return the (possibly new) head -- since the head itself
can  change,  and  C  has  no  references,  the  caller  must  reassign  head  =  insertAtHead(head,  5);
rather than expecting the function to update it silently.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 31

6.3 Reversing a linked list
This is one of the most-asked warm-up questions in real interviews, precisely because it tests whether
you actually understand pointers or have only memorized array code.
Iterative (track three pointers -- previous, current, and next-to-visit):
Node* reverseList(Node* head) {
    Node* prev = NULL;
    Node* curr = head;
    while (curr != NULL) {
        Node* nextNode = curr->next;  // save before overwriting curr->next
        curr->next = prev;
        prev = curr;
        curr = nextNode;
    }
    return prev;   // prev ends up as the new head
}
Trace on 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> NULL:
stepprevcurrcurr->next before
list state after
rewiring
1NULL121 -> NULL
21232 -> 1 -> NULL
323NULL3 -> 2 -> 1 -> NULL
Recursive (reverse everything after the head, then attach the head to the end):
Node* reverseListRecursive(Node* head) {
    if (head == NULL || head->next == NULL) return head;
    Node* newHead = reverseListRecursive(head->next);
    head->next->next = head;
    head->next = NULL;
    return newHead;
}
6.4 Doubly linked lists
Each node also stores a pointer back to the previous node, which makes backward traversal and O(1)
deletion  (given  a  node  pointer)  possible,  at  the  cost  of  one  extra  pointer's  worth  of  memory  per  node
and more bookkeeping on every insert/delete.
typedef struct DNode {
    int data;
    struct DNode* prev;
    struct DNode* next;
} DNode;
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 32

6.5 Floyd's cycle detection (the "tortoise and hare")
Problem: does this linked list loop back on itself instead of ending in NULL? Use two pointers moving
at different speeds -- if there is a cycle, the faster one will eventually lap the slower one and they will
meet.
#include <stdbool.h>
bool hasCycle(Node* head) {
    Node* slow = head;
    Node* fast = head;
    while (fast != NULL && fast->next != NULL) {
        slow = slow->next;          // moves 1 step
        fast = fast->next->next;    // moves 2 steps
        if (slow == fast) return true;
    }
    return false;
}
If there is no cycle, fast simply reaches NULL and the loop ends normally. If there is a cycle, fast is
always  gaining  exactly  one  extra  step  on  slow  per  iteration  once  both  are  inside  the  loop,  so  it  is
guaranteed to eventually land on slow -- it cannot skip past it, since the gap between them shrinks by
exactly one node each iteration.
6.6 Finding the middle node
The same slow/fast idea, without checking for equality: when fast reaches the end, slow is exactly at
the midpoint, because it has covered half the distance.
Node* findMiddle(Node* head) {
    Node* slow = head;
    Node* fast = head;
    while (fast != NULL && fast->next != NULL) {
        slow = slow->next;
        fast = fast->next->next;
    }
    return slow;
}
6.7 Merging two sorted linked lists
Uses  a  "dummy  head"  trick  that  removes  the  need  for  special-casing  the  very  first  node  --  a  pattern
worth memorizing, since it shows up constantly in linked list problems.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 33

Node* mergeTwoLists(Node* l1, Node* l2) {
    Node dummy;
    Node* tail = &dummy;
    while (l1 != NULL && l2 != NULL) {
        if (l1->data <= l2->data) {
            tail->next = l1;
            l1 = l1->next;
        } else {
            tail->next = l2;
            l2 = l2->next;
        }
        tail = tail->next;
    }
    tail->next = (l1 != NULL) ? l1 : l2;   // attach whichever list still has nodes
    return dummy.next;                      // dummy.next is the real head
}

7. Stacks and Queues
7.1 Stack -- Last In, First Out (LIFO)
A stack only allows adding and removing from one end (the "top"), like a stack of plates -- the last plate
you put down is the first one you pick back up. Both operations are O(1).
Array-based implementation:
#include <stdbool.h>
#define MAX 100
typedef struct {
    int data[MAX];
    int top;
} Stack;
void initStack(Stack* s) {
    s->top = -1;
}
bool isEmpty(Stack* s) {
    return s->top == -1;
}
void push(Stack* s, int val) {
    if (s->top == MAX - 1) return;   // full
    s->data[++(s->top)] = val;
}
int pop(Stack* s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) return -1;
    return s->data[(s->top)--];
}
int peek(Stack* s) {
    if (isEmpty(s)) return -1;
    return s->data[s->top];
}
Linked-list-based implementation (no fixed size limit):
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 35

typedef struct StackNode {
    int data;
    struct StackNode* next;
} StackNode;
StackNode* top = NULL;
void push(int val) {
    StackNode* newNode = (StackNode*) malloc(sizeof(StackNode));
    newNode->data = val;
    newNode->next = top;
    top = newNode;
}
int pop() {
    if (top == NULL) return -1;
    StackNode* temp = top;
    int val = temp->data;
    top = top->next;
    free(temp);
    return val;
}
7.2 Application: balanced parentheses
Push  every  opening  bracket.  On  a  closing  bracket,  it  must  match  whatever  is  currently  on  top  of  the
stack -- if it does not, or the stack is empty when a closing bracket arrives, the string is unbalanced.
#include <string.h>
bool isBalanced(char s[]) {
    char stack[100];
    int top = -1;
    int n = strlen(s);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        char c = s[i];
        if (c == '(' || c == '[' || c == '{') {
            stack[++top] = c;
        } else {
            if (top == -1) return false;
            char open = stack[top--];
            if ((c == ')' && open != '(') ||
                (c == ']' && open != '[') ||
                (c == '}' && open != '{')) {
                return false;
            }
        }
    }
    return top == -1;   // must end with nothing left open
}
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 36

7.3 Application: next greater element (the monotonic stack pattern)
Problem:  for  every  element,  find  the  next  element  to  its  right  that  is  bigger.  A  brute  force  check  for
every pair is O(n^2). A monotonic stack (a stack kept always increasing or always decreasing) solves
it in O(n).
void nextGreaterElement(int arr[], int n, int result[]) {
    int stack[100];
    int top = -1;
    for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        while (top != -1 && stack[top] <= arr[i]) {
            top--;   // pop values that can never be the answer for anyone to their left
        }
        result[i] = (top == -1) ? -1 : stack[top];
        stack[++top] = arr[i];
    }
}
Scanning right to left, the stack always holds a decreasing sequence of "candidates." Anything smaller
than or equal to the current element can never be the next-greater answer for anything further left (the
current element is a better, closer candidate), so it is permanently discarded. Each element is pushed
once and popped at most once, which is what makes the whole scan O(n) despite the inner while loop.
7.4 Queue -- First In, First Out (FIFO)
A queue only adds at the "rear" and removes from the "front," like a line of people -- first one in line is
the first one served.
Circular array implementation (avoids wasting slots as a plain array queue would):
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 37

#define QMAX 100
typedef struct {
    int data[QMAX];
    int front, rear, size;
} Queue;
void initQueue(Queue* q) {
    q->front = 0;
    q->rear = -1;
    q->size = 0;
}
void enqueue(Queue* q, int val) {
    if (q->size == QMAX) return;    // full
    q->rear = (q->rear + 1) % QMAX;
    q->data[q->rear] = val;
    q->size++;
}
int dequeue(Queue* q) {
    if (q->size == 0) return -1;    // empty
    int val = q->data[q->front];
    q->front = (q->front + 1) % QMAX;
    q->size--;
    return val;
}
The % QMAX is what makes it "circular" -- once rear reaches the last slot, the next enqueue wraps back
around to index 0, reusing space that earlier dequeues freed up, instead of needing to shift everything
or running out of room permanently.
Linked-list-based implementation (no fixed size limit):
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 38

typedef struct QNode {
    int data;
    struct QNode* next;
} QNode;
typedef struct {
    QNode *front, *rear;
} LinkedQueue;
void lqEnqueue(LinkedQueue* q, int val) {
    QNode* newNode = (QNode*) malloc(sizeof(QNode));
    newNode->data = val;
    newNode->next = NULL;
    if (q->rear == NULL) {
        q->front = q->rear = newNode;
        return;
    }
    q->rear->next = newNode;
    q->rear = newNode;
}
int lqDequeue(LinkedQueue* q) {
    if (q->front == NULL) return -1;
    QNode* temp = q->front;
    int val = temp->data;
    q->front = q->front->next;
    if (q->front == NULL) q->rear = NULL;
    free(temp);
    return val;
}
7.5 Deque (double-ended queue)
A  deque  allows  insertion  and  removal  from  both  ends  in  O(1).  It  generalizes  both  the  stack  and  the
queue, and is the standard tool behind the "sliding window maximum" family of problems (maintaining a
monotonic  deque  of  candidate  maximums  as  a  window  slides).  You  will  meet  this  pattern  again  in
Chapter 18.

8. Trees and Binary Search Trees
8.1 Terminology
A  tree  is  a  hierarchical  structure  of  nodes:  one  root  at  the  top,  each  node  has  some  number  of
children, a node with no children is a leaf. A binary tree restricts every node to at most two children,
conventionally called left and right. The height of a tree is the number of nodes on the longest path
from  root  to  a  leaf  (so  an  empty  tree  has  height  0,  a  single  node  has  height  1)  --  this  is  the  same
convention LeetCode uses for "maximum depth" problems. The depth of a specific node is how many
nodes lie on the path from the root down to it, inclusive.
typedef struct TreeNode {
    int data;
    struct TreeNode* left;
    struct TreeNode* right;
} TreeNode;
TreeNode* newTreeNode(int val) {
    TreeNode* node = (TreeNode*) malloc(sizeof(TreeNode));
    node->data = val;
    node->left = node->right = NULL;
    return node;
}
8.2 The three depth-first traversals
All three share the same shape -- recurse left, recurse right, visit the node -- and differ only in when the
current node is printed relative to its two subtrees.
void inorder(TreeNode* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    inorder(root->left);
    printf("%d ", root->data);
    inorder(root->right);
}
void preorder(TreeNode* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    printf("%d ", root->data);
    preorder(root->left);
    preorder(root->right);
}
void postorder(TreeNode* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    postorder(root->left);
    postorder(root->right);
    printf("%d ", root->data);
}
Why  these  three  names  and  orders  matter:  inorder  traversal  of  a  binary  search  tree  specifically
visits nodes in sorted order (Section 8.4 explains why) -- extremely useful to know cold. Preorder is the
natural  order  for  copying  a  tree  (you  visit  a  node  before  its  children,  so  you  always  create  a  parent
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 40

before  its  kids).  Postorder  is  the  natural  order  for  deleting  a  tree  or  evaluating  an  expression  tree
(children fully processed before the parent needs their results).
8.3 Level order traversal (breadth-first)
Depth-first traversals use the call stack implicitly. Level order visits the tree row by row, which requires
an explicit queue instead.
#define QCAP 1000
void levelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return;
    TreeNode* queue[QCAP];
    int front = 0, rear = 0;
    queue[rear++] = root;
    while (front < rear) {
        TreeNode* curr = queue[front++];
        printf("%d ", curr->data);
        if (curr->left != NULL)  queue[rear++] = curr->left;
        if (curr->right != NULL) queue[rear++] = curr->right;
    }
}
Every node is enqueued exactly once and dequeued exactly once, so this is O(n) time and, in the worst
case (a very wide tree), O(n) space for the queue.
8.4 Height of a tree
int height(TreeNode* root) {
    if (root == NULL) return 0;
    int leftHeight = height(root->left);
    int rightHeight = height(root->right);
    return 1 + (leftHeight > rightHeight ? leftHeight : rightHeight);
}
A small but important habit: this function computes the height of the whole subtree rooted at root by
combining  the  heights  of  its  two  children  --  almost  every  tree  problem  in  this  book  ("diameter,"  "is
balanced,"  "max  path  sum")  follows  this  same  shape:  recurse  on  both  children,  then  combine  their
results at the current node.
8.5 Binary Search Trees (BST)
The BST property: for every node, everything in its left subtree is smaller, and everything in its right
subtree is bigger. This one rule is what makes search, insert, and delete all O(height) instead of O(n) --
and it is exactly why an inorder traversal of a BST visits every value in sorted order: you always fully
exhaust  "everything  smaller"  (the  left  subtree)  before  visiting  the  node  itself,  then  move  on  to
"everything bigger" (the right subtree).
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 41

TreeNode* bstInsert(TreeNode* root, int val) {
    if (root == NULL) return newTreeNode(val);
    if (val < root->data) {
        root->left = bstInsert(root->left, val);
    } else if (val > root->data) {
        root->right = bstInsert(root->right, val);
    }
    return root;
}
TreeNode* bstSearch(TreeNode* root, int val) {
    if (root == NULL || root->data == val) return root;
    if (val < root->data) return bstSearch(root->left, val);
    return bstSearch(root->right, val);
}
Deletion is the operation that actually tests understanding, because it has three distinct cases:
TreeNode* findMin(TreeNode* root) {
    while (root->left != NULL) root = root->left;
    return root;
}
TreeNode* bstDelete(TreeNode* root, int val) {
    if (root == NULL) return NULL;
    if (val < root->data) {
        root->left = bstDelete(root->left, val);
    } else if (val > root->data) {
        root->right = bstDelete(root->right, val);
    } else {
        // this is the node to delete
        if (root->left == NULL) {
            TreeNode* temp = root->right;
            free(root);
            return temp;
        } else if (root->right == NULL) {
            TreeNode* temp = root->left;
            free(root);
            return temp;
        }
        // two children: swap in the inorder successor, then delete IT instead
        TreeNode* successor = findMin(root->right);
        root->data = successor->data;
        root->right = bstDelete(root->right, successor->data);
    }
    return root;
}
•  Case 1 -- no children (a leaf): just remove it.
•  Case 2 -- one child: the node is replaced by its only child.
•  Case 3 -- two children: you cannot just remove it without breaking the tree, so instead you copy
in the value that would come right after it in sorted order (the smallest value in its right subtree, found
by findMin), and then delete that node instead -- which is guaranteed to fall into case 1 or case 2,
never case 3 again.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 42

8.6 Why balance matters, briefly
All of the O(height) claims above are only as good as the height actually is. If you insert already-sorted
data into a plain BST, it degenerates into a straight line -- height O(n), and every operation becomes as
slow  as  a  linked  list.  Self-balancing  trees  (AVL  trees,  red-black  trees)  fix  this  by  rotating  nodes  after
insertions and deletions to keep the height at O(log n) no matter the insertion order. You will not usually
be  asked  to  implement  a  full  AVL  tree  in  an  interview,  but  the  core  mechanic  worth  recognizing  is  a
rotation, which re-parents a small piece of the tree in O(1) without breaking the BST property:
TreeNode* rotateRight(TreeNode* y) {
    TreeNode* x = y->left;
    TreeNode* T2 = x->right;
    x->right = y;
    y->left = T2;
    return x;   // x is the new root of this subtree
}
This is why C++'s map/set, Java's TreeMap/TreeSet, and similar "ordered" structures in most standard
libraries  guarantee  O(log  n)  operations  --  they  are  red-black  trees  under  the  hood,  doing  exactly  this
kind of rebalancing automatically.

9. Heaps and Priority Queues
9.1 What a heap is for
A heap answers one question extremely fast, over and over as data changes: "what is the minimum (or
maximum) element right now?" A min-heap keeps the smallest element always at the root, in O(1) to
peek, O(log n) to insert or remove it. This is exactly what a priority queue needs -- process items in
order of priority, where priority can change as new items arrive.
9.2 The array trick behind every heap
A heap is a complete binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, filled left to right) -- and a
complete binary tree can be stored in a plain array, with no pointers at all, using pure arithmetic to find
parent/child relationships:
for index i:
    parent index  = (i - 1) / 2
    left child     = 2*i + 1
    right child    = 2*i + 2
9.3 Min-heap: insert (sift up) and extract-min (sift down)
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 44

#define MAXHEAP 1000
typedef struct {
    int data[MAXHEAP];
    int size;
} MinHeap;
void swap(int* a, int* b) {
    int temp = *a; *a = *b; *b = temp;
}
void siftUp(MinHeap* h, int i) {
    while (i > 0) {
        int parent = (i - 1) / 2;
        if (h->data[parent] <= h->data[i]) break;
        swap(&h->data[parent], &h->data[i]);
        i = parent;
    }
}
void insert(MinHeap* h, int val) {
    h->data[h->size] = val;
    siftUp(h, h->size);
    h->size++;
}
void siftDown(MinHeap* h, int i) {
    while (1) {
        int left = 2 * i + 1, right = 2 * i + 2;
        int smallest = i;
        if (left < h->size && h->data[left] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = left;
        if (right < h->size && h->data[right] < h->data[smallest]) smallest = right;
        if (smallest == i) break;
        swap(&h->data[i], &h->data[smallest]);
        i = smallest;
    }
}
int extractMin(MinHeap* h) {
    int minVal = h->data[0];
    h->size--;
    h->data[0] = h->data[h->size];   // move the last element to the root
    siftDown(h, 0);                   // then restore the heap property
    return minVal;
}
siftUp  fixes  a  violation  by  bubbling  a  value  toward  the  root;  siftDown  fixes  a  violation  by  bubbling  a
value toward the leaves. Both only ever travel along one path from root to leaf (or leaf to root), which is
why both are O(log n) -- the height of a complete binary tree with n nodes is always O(log n).
9.4 Building a heap from an array in O(n), not O(n log n)
You could insert n elements one at a time (O(n log n) total), but there is a faster way: place all elements
in the array as-is, then sift down starting from the last non-leaf node backward to the root.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 45

void buildHeap(MinHeap* h, int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) h->data[i] = arr[i];
    h->size = n;
    for (int i = n / 2 - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        siftDown(h, i);
    }
}
This is O(n) overall, not O(n log n), because most nodes in a heap sit near the bottom and only need to
sift down a short distance -- only the few nodes near the root have far to travel, and the math works out
to a linear total rather than n times log n. (You do not need to reprove this in an interview -- just know
"heapify an array" is O(n), which often surprises people.)
9.5 Heap sort
Build a max-heap, then repeatedly swap the maximum (the root) to the end of the array and shrink the
heap by one, restoring the heap property each time. The result is a fully in-place, O(n log n) worst-case
sort using O(1) extra space.
void siftDownMax(int arr[], int n, int i) {
    while (1) {
        int left = 2 * i + 1, right = 2 * i + 2;
        int largest = i;
        if (left < n && arr[left] > arr[largest]) largest = left;
        if (right < n && arr[right] > arr[largest]) largest = right;
        if (largest == i) break;
        int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[largest]; arr[largest] = temp;
        i = largest;
    }
}
void heapSort(int arr[], int n) {
    for (int i = n / 2 - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        siftDownMax(arr, n, i);       // build max-heap
    }
    for (int i = n - 1; i > 0; i--) {
        int temp = arr[0]; arr[0] = arr[i]; arr[i] = temp;  // move max to the end
        siftDownMax(arr, i, 0);        // heap "shrinks" to size i
    }
}
9.6 The "top-K elements" pattern
Problem  shape:  find  the  K  largest  (or  smallest,  or  most  frequent)  elements  among  n  items.  Keep  a
min-heap of size K as you scan: push each new element, and whenever the heap exceeds size K, pop
the minimum. Whatever survives in the heap at the end is exactly the K largest elements seen -- and
every push/pop only costs O(log K), giving O(n log K) total, which beats sorting everything (O(n log n))
whenever K is small compared to n. This exact pattern reappears throughout Chapter 18.

10. Hashing
10.1 The idea: trading memory for near-instant lookups
A hash table lets you check "have I seen this before?" or "what value is stored under this key?" in O(1)
average  time,  instead  of  the  O(n)  a  linear  scan  needs  or  the  O(log  n)  a  sorted  structure  needs.  The
mechanism:  a  hash  function  converts  a  key  into  an  array  index,  so  lookups  become  direct  array
access instead of a search.
int hashFunction(int key, int tableSize) {
    return ((key % tableSize) + tableSize) % tableSize;   // handles negative keys too
}
10.2 Collisions, and chaining as the fix
Two  different  keys  can  hash  to  the  same  index  --  this  is  a  collision,  and  it  is  unavoidable  once  you
have more possible keys than table slots (a fact known as the pigeonhole principle). Chaining handles
this  by  letting  each  slot  hold  a  linked  list  of  every  entry  that  landed  there,  so  a  collision  just  means  a
slightly longer list to scan, not a broken table.
#define TABLE_SIZE 101
typedef struct HashNode {
    int key;
    int value;
    struct HashNode* next;
} HashNode;
HashNode* table[TABLE_SIZE];   // global array -- automatically all NULL at start
void hashInsert(int key, int value) {
    int idx = hashFunction(key, TABLE_SIZE);
    HashNode* newNode = (HashNode*) malloc(sizeof(HashNode));
    newNode->key = key;
    newNode->value = value;
    newNode->next = table[idx];   // insert at the head of this bucket's list
    table[idx] = newNode;
}
int hashSearch(int key) {
    int idx = hashFunction(key, TABLE_SIZE);
    HashNode* curr = table[idx];
    while (curr != NULL) {
        if (curr->key == key) return curr->value;
        curr = curr->next;
    }
    return -1;   // not found
}
The alternative approach, open addressing (most commonly linear probing), keeps everything in one
flat array with no linked lists: on a collision, it just checks the next slot, and the next, until an empty one
is found. It saves the memory overhead of linked-list pointers but requires careful handling of deletions
(a naive delete would break future searches, since a "hole" looks the same as "never occupied").
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 48

10.3 Load factor and resizing
Load  factor  =  (number  of  entries)  /  (table  size).  As  the  load  factor  climbs,  chains  get  longer  and
average lookup time drifts away from O(1) toward O(n). Real hash table implementations monitor this
and rehash into a bigger table (typically doubling) once the load factor crosses a threshold (commonly
around 0.7), which keeps the amortized cost of operations at O(1) -- the same doubling-and-amortizing
argument from Section 2.8 applies here too.
10.4 Classic problem: Two Sum via hashing
The array version from Chapter 3 assumed the array was sorted to use two pointers. Hashing removes
that requirement entirely, at the cost of O(n) extra space:
bool twoSumHashed(int arr[], int n, int target, int* idx1, int* idx2) {
    for (int i = 0; i < table_size_placeholder; i++) { }  // (see note below)
    return false;
}
In practice you would use a generic hash map keyed by value (not the fixed-size int-keyed table above,
which  was  written  for  teaching  the  mechanism)  --  but  the  algorithm  is  exactly  one  pass:  for  each
element, compute target - arr[i], check if that complement is already in the map, and if not, insert
the current element before moving on. Because each map operation is O(1) average, the whole scan is
O(n) instead of the O(n^2) that checking every pair would cost.
10.5 Classic problem: first non-repeating character
When keys are a small, known range (like ASCII characters), a plain array indexed directly by the key
works as an even simpler "hash table" than a general-purpose one -- you saw this exact idea already in
the sliding-window example in Chapter 3.
char firstNonRepeatingChar(char s[]) {
    int freq[256] = {0};
    int n = strlen(s);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        freq[(unsigned char)s[i]]++;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (freq[(unsigned char)s[i]] == 1) return s[i];
    }
    return '\0';   // none found
}

11. Tries
11.1 What problem a trie solves
A trie (from "retrieval," usually pronounced "try") stores a set of strings so that checking "does this word
exist?" or "does any word start with this prefix?" costs O(L), where L is the length of the word or prefix --
completely  independent  of  how  many  words  are  stored.  Compare  that  to  scanning  a  list  of  n  words,
each up to L characters: O(n * L). For prefix-heavy problems (autocomplete, dictionaries, word games),
a trie is a decisive improvement.
11.2 Structure
Each node holds an array of child pointers -- one slot per possible next character -- plus a flag marking
"a word ends here." For lowercase English letters, that is 26 slots per node.
#include <stdbool.h>
#define ALPHABET_SIZE 26
typedef struct TrieNode {
    struct TrieNode* children[ALPHABET_SIZE];
    bool isEndOfWord;
} TrieNode;
TrieNode* createTrieNode() {
    TrieNode* node = (TrieNode*) malloc(sizeof(TrieNode));
    node->isEndOfWord = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < ALPHABET_SIZE; i++) node->children[i] = NULL;
    return node;
}
11.3 Insert, search, and prefix search
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 51

void trieInsert(TrieNode* root, char word[]) {
    TrieNode* curr = root;
    int len = strlen(word);
    for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
        int idx = word[i] - 'a';
        if (curr->children[idx] == NULL) {
            curr->children[idx] = createTrieNode();
        }
        curr = curr->children[idx];
    }
    curr->isEndOfWord = true;
}
bool trieSearch(TrieNode* root, char word[]) {
    TrieNode* curr = root;
    int len = strlen(word);
    for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
        int idx = word[i] - 'a';
        if (curr->children[idx] == NULL) return false;
        curr = curr->children[idx];
    }
    return curr->isEndOfWord;   // must actually END here, not just pass through
}
bool trieStartsWith(TrieNode* root, char prefix[]) {
    TrieNode* curr = root;
    int len = strlen(prefix);
    for (int i = 0; i < len; i++) {
        int idx = prefix[i] - 'a';
        if (curr->children[idx] == NULL) return false;
        curr = curr->children[idx];
    }
    return true;   // walked the whole prefix without hitting a dead end
}
Notice trieSearch and trieStartsWith share almost identical code -- the only difference is the final
check. trieSearch additionally requires isEndOfWord, because "car" existing as a prefix of "cart" does
not mean "car" itself was ever inserted as a complete word.
11.4 The memory tradeoff
A trie can use more memory than a plain hash set holding the same words, since every node reserves
space for all 26 possible children even if only one or two are used. This is the standard tradeoff: tries
win  decisively  on  prefix-based  queries,  and  lose  on  raw  memory  efficiency  compared  to  a  hash  set
when you only ever need exact-match lookups.

12. Graphs -- Representation, BFS, DFS
12.1 Terminology
A graph is a set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. An edge can be directed (one-way, like a
Twitter follow) or undirected (two-way, like a Facebook friendship), and weighted (the edge carries a
cost,  like  road  distance)  or  unweighted.  Trees  (Chapter  8)  are  actually  a  special  case  of  graphs  --  a
connected graph with no cycles.
12.2 Representations
Adjacency list -- each vertex keeps a list of its neighbors:
#define MAXV 1000
typedef struct AdjNode {
    int vertex;
    struct AdjNode* next;
} AdjNode;
AdjNode* adjList[MAXV];
bool visited[MAXV];
void addEdge(int u, int v) {
    AdjNode* node1 = (AdjNode*) malloc(sizeof(AdjNode));
    node1->vertex = v;
    node1->next = adjList[u];
    adjList[u] = node1;
    AdjNode* node2 = (AdjNode*) malloc(sizeof(AdjNode));   // remove this half for
    node2->vertex = u;                                       // a DIRECTED graph
    node2->next = adjList[v];
    adjList[v] = node2;
}
Adjacency matrix -- a V x V grid where matrix[u][v] = 1 (or the weight) if an edge exists:
int adjMatrix[MAXV][MAXV];
RepresentationSpaceCheck if edge (u,v) existsIterate neighbors of u
Adjacency MatrixO(V^2)O(1)O(V)
Adjacency ListO(V+E)O(degree of u)O(degree of u)
Most real-world and interview graphs are sparse (E is much smaller than V^2, like a social network --
most  people  are  not  friends  with  most  other  people),  which  is  exactly  where  adjacency  lists  win
decisively on space and neighbor iteration.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 53

12.3 Breadth-First Search (BFS)
BFS  explores  a  graph  in  expanding  "rings"  outward  from  the  start  --  visiting  everything  1  edge  away,
then everything 2 edges away, and so on. This ring-by-ring order is exactly why BFS finds the shortest
path in an unweighted graph: the first time you reach any vertex is guaranteed to be via the fewest
possible edges.
void bfs(int start, int V) {
    int queue[MAXV];
    int front = 0, rear = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) visited[i] = false;
    visited[start] = true;
    queue[rear++] = start;
    while (front < rear) {
        int u = queue[front++];
        printf("%d ", u);
        for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
            int v = curr->vertex;
            if (!visited[v]) {
                visited[v] = true;    // mark visited when QUEUED, not when dequeued
                queue[rear++] = v;
            }
        }
    }
}
Marking  visited  at  the  moment  a  vertex  is  added  to  the  queue  (not  when  it  is  later  processed)  is  a
small but important detail -- without it, the same vertex could be queued multiple times through different
paths before it is ever dequeued.
12.4 Depth-First Search (DFS)
DFS explores as far as possible down one path before backtracking, in contrast to BFS's ring-by-ring
approach. It has a natural recursive form (using the call stack implicitly) and an equivalent iterative form
(using an explicit stack).
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 54

void dfsRecursive(int u) {
    visited[u] = true;
    printf("%d ", u);
    for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
        int v = curr->vertex;
        if (!visited[v]) {
            dfsRecursive(v);
        }
    }
}
void dfsIterative(int start, int V) {
    int stack[MAXV];
    int top = -1;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) visited[i] = false;
    stack[++top] = start;
    while (top != -1) {
        int u = stack[top--];
        if (visited[u]) continue;   // may have been pushed more than once
        visited[u] = true;
        printf("%d ", u);
        for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
            int v = curr->vertex;
            if (!visited[v]) stack[++top] = v;
        }
    }
}
Both BFS and DFS are O(V + E): every vertex is processed once, and every edge is examined exactly
once (twice for an undirected graph, once from each endpoint), which is still O(E) overall.
12.5 Application: counting connected components
Run BFS or DFS from any unvisited vertex, marking everything it reaches; each time you must start a
fresh search from a still-unvisited vertex, that is one more component.
int countComponents(int V) {
    int count = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) visited[i] = false;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        if (!visited[i]) {
            count++;
            dfsRecursive(i);
        }
    }
    return count;
}
12.6 Application: cycle detection
Undirected graph -- track the parent you arrived from, so you do not mistake "the edge you just came
in on" for a cycle:
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 55

bool hasCycleUtil(int u, int parent) {
    visited[u] = true;
    for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
        int v = curr->vertex;
        if (!visited[v]) {
            if (hasCycleUtil(v, u)) return true;
        } else if (v != parent) {
            return true;   // reached an already-visited vertex that ISN'T our parent
        }
    }
    return false;
}
Directed  graph  needs  a  different  marker,  because  revisiting  an  already-fully  processed  vertex  is
completely normal in a directed graph (two different paths can legitimately lead to the same vertex) --
what actually signals a cycle is revisiting a vertex that is still open on the current DFS path:
bool hasCycleDirectedUtil(int u, bool vis[], bool onPath[]) {
    vis[u] = true;
    onPath[u] = true;
    for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
        int v = curr->vertex;
        if (!vis[v]) {
            if (hasCycleDirectedUtil(v, vis, onPath)) return true;
        } else if (onPath[v]) {
            return true;   // v is an ancestor on the current path -> cycle
        }
    }
    onPath[u] = false;   // done with u -- remove it from the current path
    return false;
}
