13. Advanced Graph Algorithms
13.1 Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
What it answers, fast: "are these two elements in the same group?" and "merge these two groups into
one,"  both  in  almost  O(1)  time  (technically  O(alpha(n)),  an  essentially-constant  function  called  the
inverse  Ackermann  function).  This  is  the  engine  behind  Kruskal's  algorithm  below,  and  behind  many
"group the connected things" problems on its own.
int parent[MAXV], rank_[MAXV];
void makeSet(int V) {
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        parent[i] = i;
        rank_[i] = 0;
    }
}
int find(int x) {
    if (parent[x] != x) {
        parent[x] = find(parent[x]);   // path compression: flatten the tree
    }
    return parent[x];
}
void unionSets(int x, int y) {
    int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y);
    if (rootX == rootY) return;
    if (rank_[rootX] < rank_[rootY]) {
        parent[rootX] = rootY;
    } else if (rank_[rootX] > rank_[rootY]) {
        parent[rootY] = rootX;
    } else {
        parent[rootY] = rootX;
        rank_[rootX]++;
    }
}
Two  optimizations  make  this  almost-constant-time:  path  compression  (every  node  find  visits  gets
pointed straight at the root, so future lookups are instant) and union by rank (always attach the shorter
tree under the taller one, so trees stay flat). Neither alone is enough; together, they give the near-O(1)
guarantee.
13.2 Minimum Spanning Tree -- Kruskal's algorithm
Problem:  connect  all  vertices  with  the  minimum  total  edge  weight,  using  no  cycles.  Kruskal's
approach: sort all edges by weight, and greedily add the cheapest edge that does not create a cycle
(checked  instantly  with  Union-Find),  stopping  once  V-1  edges  have  been  chosen  (exactly  enough  to
connect V vertices with no cycle).
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 57

typedef struct Edge {
    int u, v, weight;
} Edge;
int compareEdges(const void* a, const void* b) {
    return ((Edge*)a)->weight - ((Edge*)b)->weight;
}
int kruskalMST(Edge edges[], int E, int V) {
    qsort(edges, E, sizeof(Edge), compareEdges);
    makeSet(V);
    int totalWeight = 0, edgesUsed = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < E && edgesUsed < V - 1; i++) {
        int u = edges[i].u, v = edges[i].v;
        if (find(u) != find(v)) {
            unionSets(u, v);
            totalWeight += edges[i].weight;
            edgesUsed++;
        }
    }
    return totalWeight;
}
13.3 Minimum Spanning Tree -- Prim's algorithm
Prim's  approach:  grow  a  single  tree  one  vertex  at  a  time,  always  attaching  the  cheapest  edge  that
connects a new vertex to the tree so far. This O(V^2) version is simplest to read; a heap-based version
brings it down to O(E log V) for sparse graphs.
#include <limits.h>
int primMST(int graph[][MAXV], int V) {
    int key[MAXV];
    bool inMST[MAXV];
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) { key[i] = INT_MAX; inMST[i] = false; }
    key[0] = 0;
    int totalWeight = 0;
    for (int count = 0; count < V; count++) {
        int u = -1;
        for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
            if (!inMST[i] && (u == -1 || key[i] < key[u])) u = i;
        }
        inMST[u] = true;
        totalWeight += key[u];
        for (int v = 0; v < V; v++) {
            if (graph[u][v] && !inMST[v] && graph[u][v] < key[v]) {
                key[v] = graph[u][v];
            }
        }
    }
    return totalWeight;
}
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 58

13.4 Dijkstra's shortest path
Problem:  shortest  distance  from  one  source  vertex  to  every  other  vertex,  with  non-negative  edge
weights. The structure mirrors Prim's closely -- the difference is that dist[v] tracks distance from the
source, not the cheapest edge to the tree.
void dijkstra(int graph[][MAXV], int V, int src, int dist[]) {
    bool visited[MAXV];
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) { dist[i] = INT_MAX; visited[i] = false; }
    dist[src] = 0;
    for (int count = 0; count < V - 1; count++) {
        int u = -1;
        for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
            if (!visited[i] && (u == -1 || dist[i] < dist[u])) u = i;
        }
        if (dist[u] == INT_MAX) break;
        visited[u] = true;
        for (int v = 0; v < V; v++) {
            if (graph[u][v] && !visited[v] && dist[u] + graph[u][v] < dist[v]) {
                dist[v] = dist[u] + graph[u][v];
            }
        }
    }
}
This O(V^2) version suits dense graphs or small V. For a large sparse graph, replace the linear "find the
closest unvisited vertex" scan with a min-heap keyed by distance, bringing the total to O((V + E) log V)
-- the same asymptotic improvement a heap gives any "repeatedly grab the minimum" algorithm.
Important:  Dijkstra's  algorithm  assumes  non-negative  weights.  A  negative  edge  can  make  its  greedy
"never revisit a finalized vertex" assumption wrong.
13.5 Bellman-Ford (handles negative weights)
Relax  every  edge,  V-1  times  over.  The  shortest  path  between  any  two  vertices  can  use  at  most  V-1
edges (no simple path repeats a vertex), so V-1 full passes are guaranteed enough for the distances to
converge.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 59

bool bellmanFord(Edge edges[], int E, int V, int src, int dist[]) {
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) dist[i] = INT_MAX;
    dist[src] = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < V - 1; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < E; j++) {
            int u = edges[j].u, v = edges[j].v, w = edges[j].weight;
            if (dist[u] != INT_MAX && dist[u] + w < dist[v]) {
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w;
            }
        }
    }
    for (int j = 0; j < E; j++) {           // one extra pass to detect negative cycles
        int u = edges[j].u, v = edges[j].v, w = edges[j].weight;
        if (dist[u] != INT_MAX && dist[u] + w < dist[v]) {
            return false;   // still improving after V-1 passes -> negative cycle
        }
    }
    return true;
}
13.6 Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs shortest path)
Answers "shortest path between every pair of vertices" at once, in O(V^3) -- a good fit when V is a few
hundred or fewer and you need every pairwise distance, not just distances from one source.
void floydWarshall(int dist[][MAXV], int V) {
    // dist[i][i] = 0, dist[i][j] = edge weight if it exists, else a large INF value
    for (int k = 0; k < V; k++) {
        for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < V; j++) {
                if (dist[i][k] + dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]) {
                    dist[i][j] = dist[i][k] + dist[k][j];
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
The idea: after the k-th outer iteration, dist[i][j] holds the shortest path from i to j allowed to pass
only through intermediate vertices 0..k. By the time k reaches V-1, every vertex has been allowed as
an intermediate, so the answer is final.
13.7 Topological sort (Kahn's algorithm)
Problem: order vertices of a directed acyclic graph so that every edge points from earlier to later in
the  order  (used  for  "this  must  happen  before  that"  scheduling,  like  course  prerequisites).  Kahn's
algorithm  processes  vertices  with  no  remaining  unprocessed  prerequisites  first,  using  in-degree
(number of incoming edges) as the tracker.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 60

void topologicalSort(int V) {
    int inDegree[MAXV] = {0};
    for (int u = 0; u < V; u++) {
        for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
            inDegree[curr->vertex]++;
        }
    }
    int queue[MAXV];
    int front = 0, rear = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) {
        if (inDegree[i] == 0) queue[rear++] = i;   // no prerequisites -> can start now
    }
    int order[MAXV], idx = 0;
    while (front < rear) {
        int u = queue[front++];
        order[idx++] = u;
        for (AdjNode* curr = adjList[u]; curr != NULL; curr = curr->next) {
            int v = curr->vertex;
            inDegree[v]--;                   // one prerequisite of v is now done
            if (inDegree[v] == 0) queue[rear++] = v;
        }
    }
    if (idx != V) {
        printf("Cycle detected -- no valid topological order exists\n");
    } else {
        for (int i = 0; i < idx; i++) printf("%d ", order[i]);
    }
}
If  idx  never  reaches  V,  some  vertices'  in-degree  never  dropped  to  zero  --  meaning  they  were  stuck
waiting on each other in a cycle, which is exactly why this same algorithm doubles as a directed-cycle
detector.

14. Greedy Algorithms
14.1 The idea
A greedy algorithm builds a solution one choice at a time, always taking whatever looks best right now,
and  never  revisiting  that  decision.  This  is  much  cheaper  than  exploring  every  possibility  (Chapter  5's
backtracking) or solving every subproblem (Chapter 15's dynamic programming) -- but it only produces
the  correct  answer  for  problems  where  the  locally-best  choice  is  provably  always  part  of  some
globally-best  solution.  Recognizing  whether  a  problem  actually  has  this  property  is  the  real  skill;  the
code itself is usually short.
14.2 Activity selection
Problem:  given  activities  with  start  and  finish  times,  pick  the  maximum  number  that  do  not  overlap.
Greedy rule: sort by finish time, and always take the next activity whose start is not before the last one
you accepted.
typedef struct Activity {
    int start, finish;
} Activity;
int compareActivities(const void* a, const void* b) {
    return ((Activity*)a)->finish - ((Activity*)b)->finish;
}
int maxActivities(Activity acts[], int n) {
    qsort(acts, n, sizeof(Activity), compareActivities);
    int count = 1;
    int lastFinish = acts[0].finish;
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        if (acts[i].start >= lastFinish) {
            count++;
            lastFinish = acts[i].finish;
        }
    }
    return count;
}
Why finishing earliest is provably the right first choice: whichever activity finishes soonest leaves
the most possible room for everything after it. Any optimal solution that picked a different first activity
can  always  be  rewritten  to  start  with  the  earliest-finishing  one  instead,  without  making  the  total  count
any worse -- this kind of argument is called an "exchange argument," and it is the standard way greedy
correctness gets justified.
14.3 Fractional knapsack
Problem: fill a bag of limited weight capacity with items (each with a weight and a value) to maximize
total value, where items can be split into fractions. Greedy rule: always take as much as possible of the
item with the best value-to-weight ratio first.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 62

typedef struct Item {
    int weight, value;
} Item;
int compareByRatio(const void* a, const void* b) {
    Item* itemA = (Item*)a;
    Item* itemB = (Item*)b;
    double ratioA = (double) itemA->value / itemA->weight;
    double ratioB = (double) itemB->value / itemB->weight;
    if (ratioB > ratioA) return 1;
    if (ratioB < ratioA) return -1;
    return 0;
}
double fractionalKnapsack(Item items[], int n, int capacity) {
    qsort(items, n, sizeof(Item), compareByRatio);
    double totalValue = 0.0;
    int remaining = capacity;
    for (int i = 0; i < n && remaining > 0; i++) {
        if (items[i].weight <= remaining) {
            totalValue += items[i].value;
            remaining -= items[i].weight;
        } else {
            totalValue += items[i].value * ((double) remaining / items[i].weight);
            remaining = 0;
        }
    }
    return totalValue;
}
This greedy approach only works because items are splittable. The moment items must be taken
whole  (the  "0/1  Knapsack"  version),  the  best  ratio  is  no  longer  guaranteed  to  lead  to  the  best  total  --
that version genuinely needs Dynamic Programming, which is exactly the first full example in Chapter
15. Knapsack is the single clearest example in this entire book of "a tiny change in the rules moves a
problem from one entire technique to a completely different one."
14.4 Huffman coding, briefly
Problem: compress data by giving frequently-used characters shorter binary codes and rare characters
longer ones, in a way that no code is a prefix of another (so a decoder can tell where one code ends
and the next begins). Greedy rule: repeatedly take the two least-frequent remaining nodes and merge
them  into  a  new  node  (with  combined  frequency),  until  one  tree  remains  --  notice  this  is  exactly  the
min-heap "always grab the smallest" pattern from Chapter 9, applied greedily. The path from the root to
each  character's  leaf  becomes  its  code;  more  frequent  characters  end  up  closer  to  the  root  and  get
shorter codes.

15. Dynamic Programming
15.1 Why DP exists -- go back to the recursion tree
Look back at the Fibonacci recursion tree in Section 5.2. fib(2) was computed three separate times.
Dynamic  Programming  is  simply  the  idea  of  remembering  the  answer  to  a  subproblem  the  first  time
you  compute  it,  so  every  subsequent  request  for  that  same  subproblem  is  a  free  lookup  instead  of
repeated work. It applies exactly when a problem has:
•  Overlapping subproblems: the same smaller subproblem gets solved repeatedly (as seen in the
Fibonacci tree).
•  Optimal substructure: the optimal answer to the whole problem can be built from optimal
answers to its subproblems.
15.2 Two ways to remember: memoization vs tabulation
Memoization (top-down): keep the natural recursive structure, but check a cache before recomputing,
and fill the cache after computing.
int memo[100];
bool computed[100] = {false};
int fibMemo(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    if (computed[n]) return memo[n];
    memo[n] = fibMemo(n - 1) + fibMemo(n - 2);
    computed[n] = true;
    return memo[n];
}
Tabulation (bottom-up): flip the direction entirely -- start from the base cases and iteratively build up to
the answer, with no recursion at all.
int fibTab(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    int dp[n + 1];
    dp[0] = 0;
    dp[1] = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2];
    }
    return dp[n];
}
Space-optimized:  once  you  notice  dp[i]  only  ever  depends  on  the  previous  two  values,  there  is  no
need to keep the whole table at all.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 65

int fibOptimal(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    int prev2 = 0, prev1 = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        int curr = prev1 + prev2;
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = curr;
    }
    return prev1;
}
Four versions of the exact same problem: naive recursion is O(2^n) time; memoization and tabulation
are both O(n) time, O(n) space; the space-optimized version is O(n) time, O(1) space. This progression
-- notice the repeated work, cache it, then notice you can shrink the cache -- is the mental checklist to
run on every new DP problem.
15.3 The same shape in disguise: Climbing Stairs
Problem: you can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time; how many distinct ways to reach step n? The number of
ways to reach step n is the ways to reach step n-1 (then take one final step) plus the ways to reach step
n-2  (then  take  a  final  double  step)  --  which  is  exactly  the  Fibonacci  recurrence,  wearing  a  different
costume.
int climbStairs(int n) {
    if (n <= 2) return n;
    int prev2 = 1, prev1 = 2;
    for (int i = 3; i <= n; i++) {
        int curr = prev1 + prev2;
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = curr;
    }
    return prev1;
}
This  is  the  single  most  valuable  habit  Dynamic  Programming  rewards:  learning  to  spot  that  a
brand-new-looking problem has the same recurrence shape as one you have already solved.
15.4 0/1 Knapsack -- the first 2D DP
Problem: given item weights and values, and a capacity, choose a subset (each item taken whole or
not at all) that maximizes total value without exceeding capacity. For each item, there are exactly two
choices  --  skip  it,  or  take  it  (if  it  fits)  --  so  build  a  table  where  dp[i][w]  means  "the  best  value
achievable using only the first i items, with capacity w."
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 66

int knapsack(int weights[], int values[], int n, int capacity) {
    int dp[n + 1][capacity + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
        for (int w = 0; w <= capacity; w++) {
            if (i == 0 || w == 0) {
                dp[i][w] = 0;
            } else if (weights[i - 1] > w) {
                dp[i][w] = dp[i - 1][w];              // doesn't fit -- must skip
            } else {
                int skip = dp[i - 1][w];
                int take = values[i - 1] + dp[i - 1][w - weights[i - 1]];
                dp[i][w] = (skip > take) ? skip : take;
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[n][capacity];
}

1+4=5,  weight  1+3=4),  or  the  weight-4  item  alone  (value  5,  weight  4).  Both  reach  the  capacity-4  limit
with value 5, and no combination does better.
15.5 Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
Problem: given two strings, find the length of the longest sequence of characters that appears in both,
in the same relative order (not necessarily contiguous). dp[i][j] means "LCS length using the first i
characters of s1 and the first j characters of s2."
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 67

int lcs(char s1[], char s2[]) {
    int m = strlen(s1), n = strlen(s2);
    int dp[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j <= n; j++) {
            if (i == 0 || j == 0) {
                dp[i][j] = 0;
            } else if (s1[i - 1] == s2[j - 1]) {
                dp[i][j] = 1 + dp[i - 1][j - 1];   // matching chars extend the LCS
            } else {
                int a = dp[i - 1][j];
                int b = dp[i][j - 1];
                dp[i][j] = (a > b) ? a : b;         // best of dropping from either side
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m][n];
}
Trace with s1 = "ABC", s2 = "AC": dp[1][1] matches on 'A', giving 1. dp[3][2] eventually matches on
the final 'C', adding to the 'A' match already found, for a final LCS length of 2 (the subsequence "AC").
15.6 Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS)
Problem:  the  length  of  the  longest  subsequence  where  each  element  is  strictly  bigger  than  the  one
before. dp[i] means "length of the longest increasing subsequence that ends at index i."
int lengthOfLIS(int arr[], int n) {
    int dp[n];
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) dp[i] = 1;   // every single element is a length-1 LIS
    int best = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            if (arr[j] < arr[i] && dp[j] + 1 > dp[i]) {
                dp[i] = dp[j] + 1;
            }
        }
        if (dp[i] > best) best = dp[i];
    }
    return best;
}
This is O(n^2): for every index i, check every earlier index j. An O(n log n) approach exists (maintaining
the smallest possible "tail" value for every achievable LIS length, updated with binary search) -- worth
knowing exists, though the O(n^2) version is usually the expected first solution.
15.7 Edit distance
Problem:  minimum  number  of  insertions,  deletions,  or  substitutions  to  turn  one  string  into  another.
dp[i][j] means "edit distance between the first i characters of s1 and the first j characters of s2."
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 68

int editDistance(char s1[], char s2[]) {
    int m = strlen(s1), n = strlen(s2);
    int dp[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= m; i++) dp[i][0] = i;
    for (int j = 0; j <= n; j++) dp[0][j] = j;
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (s1[i - 1] == s2[j - 1]) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1];        // free -- characters already match
            } else {
                int insertOp = dp[i][j - 1];
                int deleteOp = dp[i - 1][j];
                int replaceOp = dp[i - 1][j - 1];
                int minOp = insertOp;
                if (deleteOp < minOp) minOp = deleteOp;
                if (replaceOp < minOp) minOp = replaceOp;
                dp[i][j] = 1 + minOp;
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m][n];
}

16. Bit Manipulation
16.1 Refresher: the bitwise operators
int a = 12;    // binary: 1100
int b = 10;    // binary: 1010
a & b;   // AND: 1000  =  8
a | b;   // OR:  1110  = 14
a ^ b;   // XOR: 0110  =  6
~a;      // NOT: flips every bit
a << 2;  // left shift:  110000 = 48  (multiplies by 2^2)
a >> 2;  // right shift: 11     =  3  (divides by 2^2, for non-negative numbers)
Bit tricks show up in interviews specifically because they solve certain problems in O(1) or O(log(value))
with essentially no extra memory, where an array-based approach would cost O(n) space.
16.2 Check if a number is a power of 2
bool isPowerOfTwo(int n) {
    return n > 0 && (n & (n - 1)) == 0;
}
A power of 2 is always a single 1 bit followed by zeros (8 = 1000). Subtracting 1 flips that single bit to 0
and every bit below it to 1 (7 = 0111). ANDing the two only produces 0 when there was exactly one bit
set to begin with.
16.3 Count set bits -- Brian Kernighan's algorithm
int countSetBits(int n) {
    int count = 0;
    while (n != 0) {
        n = n & (n - 1);   // clears the lowest set bit
        count++;
    }
    return count;
}
n & (n - 1) always clears exactly the lowest set bit (the same trick as above, just used repeatedly).
This loop runs once per set bit, not once per bit position -- so for a number with only a few set bits, it is
much faster than checking all 32 positions one at a time.
16.4 Find the single unique number (XOR trick)
Problem: every number in an array appears exactly twice, except one. Find it, in O(n) time and O(1)
space.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 71

int findUnique(int arr[], int n) {
    int result = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        result = result ^ arr[i];
    }
    return result;
}
XOR-ing any number with itself gives 0, and XOR is commutative and associative, so every pair in the
array cancels itself out no matter what order they appear in, leaving only the number with no partner.
16.5 Get, set, clear, and toggle a bit
int getBit(int n, int i)    { return (n >> i) & 1; }
int setBit(int n, int i)    { return n | (1 << i); }
int clearBit(int n, int i)  { return n & ~(1 << i); }
int toggleBit(int n, int i) { return n ^ (1 << i); }
1 << i builds a "mask" with only bit i turned on; combining that mask with &, |, or ^ against the original
number reads, sets, or flips exactly that one bit without disturbing any other.

17. Bonus: Advanced Structures (Segment Tree,
Fenwick Tree, KMP)
These three show up in harder LeetCode problems and in some product-company interview loops, but
are not universally required -- treat this chapter as a strength multiplier once Chapters 1-16 feel solid,
not a blocker before you start practicing.
17.1 Segment Tree -- range queries with updates
Problem shape: you need both "update a single value" and "sum (or min/max) over a range" to be fast,
interleaved,  on  the  same  array.  A  plain  array  makes  update  O(1)  but  range  sum  O(n);  a  prefix  sum
array makes range sum O(1) but update O(n). A segment tree makes both O(log n), by storing a binary
tree over the array where every node summarizes the range it covers.
#define MAXN 100000
int tree[4 * MAXN];
int arr[MAXN];
void build(int node, int start, int end) {
    if (start == end) {
        tree[node] = arr[start];
        return;
    }
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;
    build(2 * node, start, mid);
    build(2 * node + 1, mid + 1, end);
    tree[node] = tree[2 * node] + tree[2 * node + 1];
}
void update(int node, int start, int end, int idx, int val) {
    if (start == end) {
        arr[idx] = val;
        tree[node] = val;
        return;
    }
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;
    if (idx <= mid) update(2 * node, start, mid, idx, val);
    else update(2 * node + 1, mid + 1, end, idx, val);
    tree[node] = tree[2 * node] + tree[2 * node + 1];
}
int query(int node, int start, int end, int l, int r) {
    if (r < start || end < l) return 0;              // no overlap
    if (l <= start && end <= r) return tree[node];   // fully covered
    int mid = (start + end) / 2;
    int leftSum = query(2 * node, start, mid, l, r);
    int rightSum = query(2 * node + 1, mid + 1, end, l, r);
    return leftSum + rightSum;                         // partial overlap -- recurse
}
17.2 Fenwick Tree / Binary Indexed Tree (BIT)
A  more  compact  structure  for  prefix-sum-style  queries  (sum  only,  not  min/max)  achieving  the  same
O(log n) update and query, using far less code than a segment tree.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 73

int bit[MAXN + 1];
int n;
void bitUpdate(int idx, int delta) {
    for (; idx <= n; idx += idx & (-idx)) {
        bit[idx] += delta;
    }
}
int bitQuery(int idx) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (; idx > 0; idx -= idx & (-idx)) {
        sum += bit[idx];
    }
    return sum;
}
int bitRangeQuery(int l, int r) {
    return bitQuery(r) - bitQuery(l - 1);
}
idx & (-idx) isolates the lowest set bit of idx (the same two's-complement trick family as Chapter 16),
which is exactly what lets this structure jump straight between the O(log n) indices "responsible" for a
given prefix, instead of needing an explicit tree of pointers at all.
17.3 KMP string matching
Problem:  find  every  occurrence  of  a  pattern  inside  a  text.  The  naive  approach  re-checks  the  whole
pattern  from  scratch  after  every  mismatch,  costing  O(n*m).  KMP  precomputes  an  array  (the  "LPS"
array -- longest proper prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix) so that on a mismatch, it can skip ahead
intelligently instead of restarting, bringing the total down to O(n + m).
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 74

void computeLPS(char pattern[], int m, int lps[]) {
    int len = 0;
    lps[0] = 0;
    int i = 1;
    while (i < m) {
        if (pattern[i] == pattern[len]) {
            len++;
            lps[i] = len;
            i++;
        } else if (len != 0) {
            len = lps[len - 1];
        } else {
            lps[i] = 0;
            i++;
        }
    }
}
void kmpSearch(char text[], char pattern[]) {
    int n = strlen(text), m = strlen(pattern);
    int lps[m];
    computeLPS(pattern, m, lps);
    int i = 0, j = 0;
    while (i < n) {
        if (text[i] == pattern[j]) {
            i++;
            j++;
            if (j == m) {
                printf("Match found at index %d\n", i - j);
                j = lps[j - 1];
            }
        } else if (j != 0) {
            j = lps[j - 1];
        } else {
            i++;
        }
    }
}

•  Implement strStr() (KMP)
•  Longest Happy Prefix (LPS array, directly)

18. The LeetCode Pattern Playbook
18.1 A systematic approach to any new problem
Before  touching  a  pattern,  run  through  this  checklist  --  it  costs  two  minutes  and  prevents  most  of  the
wasted effort that comes from coding the wrong approach:
1.  Restate the problem in your own words, out loud if it's a real interview. This alone catches a
large fraction of misunderstandings before they cost you.
2.  Ask about edge cases and constraints: can the input be empty? Are there duplicates?
Negative numbers? What is the actual size of n (Chapter 2's table)?
3.  Match a pattern using the signals in this chapter.
4.  Start from brute force out loud if the pattern isn't obvious immediately, then look for the specific
inefficiency (repeated work? unnecessary nested loop?) that a pattern would remove.
5.  Code it, narrating your structure as you go.
6.  Trace it by hand on a small example before declaring you are done.
7.  State the final time and space complexity without being asked -- interviewers consistently rate
this well, and it forces one last sanity check on your own solution.
18.2 The patterns, by recognition signal
Two Pointers
Recognize  it  by:  a  sorted  array,  or  a  problem  about  pairs/triplets  that  sum  to  a  target,  or  checking  a
palindrome.  Core  idea:  two  indices  moving  toward  or  away  from  each  other  based  on  a  comparison,
covering the array in one pass. Covered in: Chapter 3. Examples: Two Sum II, 3Sum, Container With
Most Water.
Sliding Window
Recognize  it  by:  "contiguous  subarray/substring,"  "at  most/exactly  K  distinct,"  or  "longest/shortest
window satisfying X." Core idea: expand a window by moving one pointer forward, shrink it by moving
the  other,  never  restart  from  scratch.  Covered  in:  Chapter  3.  Examples:  Longest  Substring  Without
Repeating Characters, Minimum Window Substring.
Fast & Slow Pointers
Recognize  it  by:  linked  list  cycle  questions,  finding  a  middle  node,  or  any  "detect  a  repeating  state"
problem (like Happy Number). Core idea: two pointers moving at different speeds through a sequence
will meet if and only if it loops. Covered in: Chapter 6. Examples: Linked List Cycle, Happy Number,
Find the Duplicate Number.
Merge Intervals
Recognize  it  by:  an  array  of  [start, end]  pairs,  with  words  like  "merge,"  "overlapping,"  or  "insert
interval." Core idea: sort by start time, then walk through once, merging whenever the current interval's
start   is   not   after   the   previous   interval's   end.   Examples:   Merge   Intervals,   Insert   Interval,
Non-overlapping Intervals (Chapter 14).
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 76

Cyclic Sort
Recognize it by: an array containing numbers in a known range like [1, n] or [0, n-1], with "find the
missing/duplicate  number."  Core  idea:  since  each  value  maps  to  a  specific  index,  repeatedly  swap
each element toward its "correct" index in one pass -- afterward, any index whose value doesn't match
reveals the answer. Examples: Missing Number, Find All Duplicates in an Array.
In-place Linked List Reversal
Recognize  it  by:  "reverse  a  linked  list,"  or  "reverse  every  group  of  k  nodes."  Core  idea:  the
three-pointer  prev/curr/next  rewiring  from  Chapter  6,  sometimes  applied  repeatedly  to  sub-segments.
Covered in: Chapter 6. Examples: Reverse Linked List, Reverse Nodes in k-Group.
Tree BFS
Recognize  it  by:  "level  order,"  "minimum  depth,"  "zigzag  traversal,"  anything  about  tree  levels.  Core
idea: a queue, processing one full level at a time. Covered in: Chapter 8. Examples: Binary Tree Level
Order Traversal, Binary Tree Right Side View.
Tree DFS
Recognize it by: "root-to-leaf path," "path sum," anything about a specific path rather than levels. Core
idea: recurse into children, carrying state (a running sum, a path list) down the call stack. Covered in:
Chapter 8. Examples: Path Sum II, Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum.
Two Heaps
Recognize it by: "median of a running stream of numbers," needing fast access to both "the largest of
the  smaller  half"  and  "the  smallest  of  the  larger  half"  at  once.  Core  idea:  a  max-heap  for  the  smaller
half,  a  min-heap  for  the  larger  half,  kept  balanced  in  size.  Covered  in:  Chapter  9.  Examples:  Find
Median from Data Stream.
Subsets / Backtracking
Recognize  it  by:  "all  possible  subsets/permutations/combinations,"  small  n  (look  at  Chapter  2's
constraint  table  --  n  under  about  20-25  is  the  tell).  Core  idea:  choose-explore-unchoose  recursion.
Covered in: Chapter 5. Examples: Subsets, Combination Sum, Palindrome Partitioning.
Modified Binary Search
Recognize   it   by:   a   sorted   or   rotated-sorted   array,   or   a   problem   phrased   as   "find   the
minimum/maximum  X  such  that  some  condition  holds"  (binary  search  on  the  answer,  not  on  array
indices). Core idea: narrow a range in half each step based on a feasibility check. Covered in: Chapter
4.  Examples:  Search  in  Rotated  Sorted  Array,  Koko  Eating  Bananas,  Capacity  To  Ship  Packages
Within D Days.
Top K Elements
Recognize  it  by:  "K  largest/smallest/most  frequent."  Core  idea:  a  heap  of  size  K,  giving  O(n  log  K)
instead of sorting everything at O(n log n). Covered in: Chapter 9. Examples: Kth Largest Element in
an Array, Top K Frequent Elements.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 77

K-way Merge
Recognize it by: "merge K sorted lists/arrays." Core idea: a min-heap holding one candidate element
from  each  list  at  a  time;  repeatedly  pop  the  smallest  and  push  its  list's  next  element.  Covered  in:
Chapter 9. Examples: Merge K Sorted Lists, Smallest Range Covering Elements from K Lists.
Topological Sort
Recognize it by: "order/schedule with prerequisites/dependencies." Core idea: process nodes whose
prerequisites are all satisfied first, tracked via in-degree. Covered in: Chapter 13. Examples: Course
Schedule, Alien Dictionary.
Monotonic Stack
Recognize  it  by:  "next  greater/smaller  element,"  "daily  temperatures,"  anything  about  the  nearest
bigger/smaller  neighbor.  Core  idea:  a  stack  kept  always  increasing  or  decreasing,  discarding  entries
that  can  never  be  anyone's  answer  again.  Covered  in:  Chapter  7.  Examples:  Daily  Temperatures,
Largest Rectangle in Histogram.
Union-Find
Recognize  it  by:  "connected  components,"  "redundant  connection,"  any  problem  about  merging  or
grouping entities over time. Core idea: near-O(1) find/union with path compression and union by rank.
Covered in: Chapter 13. Examples: Number of Provinces, Redundant Connection, Accounts Merge.
Dynamic Programming families
Recognize   it   by:   "count   the   number   of   ways,"   "minimum/maximum   cost   to...,"   "can   you
partition/split...."  Core  idea:  identify  the  recurrence,  then  choose  memoization  or  tabulation.  Covered
in: Chapter 15. A few extra families worth naming beyond Chapter 15's table:

19. Placement Preparation Roadmap
19.1 How to structure practice over time
Practice  in  three  distinct  phases,  in  this  order  --  doing  them  out  of  order  is  the  most  common  reason
people plateau:
1.  Topic-wise (most of your prep time): work one chapter at a time, right after reading it, so the
pattern is fresh. Do not mix topics yet -- the goal here is depth on one idea at a time.
2.  Mixed, timed practice: once most topics are covered, switch to random problems with a strict
timer (45 minutes each), with no idea in advance which pattern applies. This is what actually trains
pattern recognition under pressure, which topic-wise practice cannot.
3.  Mock interviews: practice explaining your thinking out loud to another person (a friend, a
mentor, or a mock-interview platform), not just solving silently. Solving alone and solving while
narrating your thought process are different skills, and only the second one is what gets evaluated in
a real interview.

Adjust  the  pace  to  your  own  schedule  --  what  matters  is  the  order  and  the  shift  from  topic-wise  to
mixed-and-timed partway through, not hitting exactly 10 weeks.
19.3 Resume notes for an early-career / student resume
•  Lead with impact, not duties. "Built a REST API" says less than "Built a REST API handling
10,000 requests/day, cutting response time by 40%."
•  2-4 real projects, described in one line each: what it does, what you built it with, and a concrete
result or capability. Depth on a few projects beats a long, shallow list.
•  Keep it to one page for a student or early-career resume. Anything a reviewer has to hunt for
gets skipped.
•  List your competitive programming / practice profile (LeetCode, Codeforces, GitHub) if it
reflects real, sustained effort -- it gives a recruiter a concrete, checkable signal beyond the resume
text itself.
•  Tailor to the role: a pure software engineering role wants to see DSA practice, systems/project
work, and CS fundamentals front and center; a research-adjacent role wants publications or
research projects placed above generic coursework.
19.4 How to actually behave in a mock or real interview
•  Think out loud, constantly, even (especially) when stuck. An interviewer cannot evaluate
silence, and cannot give you a nudge in the right direction if they do not know where your thinking
currently is.
•  Clarify before coding: input size, duplicates, sorted or not, what to do on invalid input. This is not
stalling -- it is exactly what Section 18.1 recommends, and it consistently reads as strong signal
rather than weakness. Ask about the input size specifically -- it tells you the expected complexity
(Chapter 2).
•  State the brute force out loud first, even when you already see a better approach. Naming the
brute force, and then naming specifically what is inefficient about it, is what actually demonstrates
you understand why the optimized approach works, rather than having only memorized it.
•  Trace your code on a real example by hand once it is written -- do not just glance at it and
declare it correct.
•  State time and space complexity unprompted at the end. Interviewers consistently rate this
well, and it is a final chance to catch your own mistake.
19.5 Common mistakes that cost more than they should
•  Jumping straight into code with no stated plan.
•  Staying silent while thinking, instead of narrating.
•  Having memorized a specific solution instead of the underlying pattern -- this breaks the moment
the problem is even slightly modified from the version you practiced.
•  Ignoring the hints hidden in the stated constraints (Chapter 2's table exists specifically to train you
out of this).
•  Declaring the solution finished without testing it against even one example.
DSA in C -- From Scratch to PlacementPage 80

•  Going quiet and stuck instead of methodically re-examining which of your assumptions might be
wrong.
19.6 A short checklist for the day itself
•  Sleep normally. Do not cram a brand-new topic the night before -- at this point, review beats new
material every time.
•  Skim Chapter 18's pattern table once, as a warm-up, not as new learning.
•  For virtual interviews: test your coding environment and camera/audio beforehand, and keep
water nearby.
•  Prepare a few honest, specific questions about the team or role for when you are inevitably asked
"do you have any questions for us?"

20.4 Closing note
Everything  in  this  book  builds  on  the  four-step  loop  from  the  very  first  page:  understand,  code  it
yourself, trace it by hand, then practice under real conditions. There is no shortcut past typing the code
out and getting stuck on the practice problems -- the stuck feeling is not a sign you are doing it wrong, it
is  the  mechanism  by  which  the  pattern  actually  becomes  yours.  Work  through  it  in  order,  and  by
Chapter  19's  mixed-practice  weeks,  problems  that  would  have  looked  unfamiliar  at  the  start  will  start
resolving into patterns you already recognize on sight. That recognition, built up one chapter at a time,
is what "placement-ready" actually means.
